guidelineReferences.name: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
keyPoints: CAP with septic shock: antimicrobials within 1 hour (Strong)
CAP with sepsis (no shock): antimicrobials within 3 hours if infection persists (Weak)
30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypoperfusion; balanced crystalloids preferred
For ARDS: low tidal volume 6 mL/kg, plateau ≤30 cm H2O (Strong)
Moderate-severe ARDS: prone positioning >12 hours daily (Strong)
Stress ulcer prophylaxis for GI bleeding risk factors (Weak)
VTE prophylaxis with LMWH (Strong)

guidelineReferences.name: ATS/IDSA CAP Guidelines 2019
guidelineReferences.citation: Metlay JP, et al. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2019;200(7):e45-e67
keyPoints: Severe CAP: β-lactam + macrolide OR respiratory fluoroquinolone
ICU admission for shock or respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation

male-55-typical.name: Robert Garcia
male-55-typical.chiefComplaint: I've had a bad cough with fever for 4 days and now I'm having trouble breathing

missedConsequences.Community-Acquired Pneumonia: Untreated pneumonia progresses to sepsis, respiratory failure, ARDS, and death.
missedConsequences.Sepsis: Unrecognized sepsis leads to multiorgan failure. Early antibiotics reduce mortality.
missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: PE can present with similar symptoms. Missed PE leads to cardiovascular collapse.
missedConsequences.Lung Cancer: Pneumonia can be presenting sign of obstructing tumor. Failure to follow up imaging misses cancer.

commonPitfalls: Not calculating severity score (CURB-65 or PSI) to guide disposition
Inappropriate antibiotic selection - must cover atypicals in outpatients
Forgetting to obtain blood cultures before antibiotics in admitted patients
Not recognizing sepsis criteria even with 'just pneumonia'
Failing to arrange follow-up chest X-ray to ensure resolution (especially in smokers)

mcqs.question: The patient has RR 24, BUN 22, BP 128/78, and is confused about today's date. What is his CURB-65 score and disposition?

options.explanation: Score is higher. Confusion (1) + RR≥30... wait, RR is 24, so RR=0. BUN<20 is 0. Let's recalculate.
Confusion (1) + Urea/BUN >19 (1) + RR<30 (0) + BP ok (0) + Age <65 (0) = 2. Score ≥2 suggests hospital admission.
Score is 2, not 4. ICU criteria include shock or respiratory failure requiring ventilation.

options.text: CURB-65 doesn't apply to this patient
options.explanation: CURB-65 is specifically designed for CAP severity assessment.

mcqs.question: For inpatient (non-ICU) CAP treatment, which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?

options.explanation: Monotherapy with azithromycin is for outpatients only. Inpatients need broader coverage.

options.text: Respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin) OR beta-lactam plus macrolide
options.explanation: IDSA guidelines: Inpatient non-ICU CAP requires either a respiratory FQ alone OR beta-lactam (ceftriaxone) plus macrolide (azithromycin).
This is hospital-acquired pneumonia coverage, not CAP. Overly broad for typical CAP.
Does not cover atypical organisms (Mycoplasma, Legionella). Need macrolide or FQ coverage.

allDiagnoses.name: Community-Acquired Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Fever, productive cough, dyspnea, and hypoxia with 4-day history. Focal exam findings expected.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Bronchitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cough and URI symptoms overlap, but fever/hypoxia/focal findings suggest pneumonia.

allDiagnoses.name: Influenza
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present similarly and predispose to bacterial superinfection. Consider testing in season.

allDiagnoses.name: Sepsis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Pneumonia is most common cause of sepsis. Fever + tachycardia + tachypnea meets SIRS criteria.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause fever, dyspnea, hypoxia. Consider if risk factors present or not improving.

allDiagnoses.name: Empyema
allDiagnoses.rationale: Complicated parapneumonic effusion. Consider if not improving on antibiotics.

allDiagnoses.name: Lung Abscess
allDiagnoses.rationale: Risk with aspiration. May need prolonged antibiotics or drainage.

allDiagnoses.name: Heart Failure Exacerbation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause dyspnea and even low-grade fever. CXR will differentiate.

allDiagnoses.name: COVID-19
allDiagnoses.rationale: Always consider in current era. Test if clinically appropriate.

allDiagnoses.name: Tuberculosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Consider with prolonged symptoms, risk factors, upper lobe involvement.

male-55-typical.oldcartsNarrative: "I've been sick for about 4 days now. Started with body aches and chills, then developed this bad cough. The cough brings up greenish-yellow mucus. I've had fevers - my wife said I was at 102 last night. Today I started having trouble catching my breath, especially when I walk around. It's gotten worse today which is why I came in. The breathing trouble is probably a 6 out of 10. Nothing makes it better. I took some Tylenol which helped the fever temporarily but it keeps coming back. The cough is constant, especially at night. It hurts on my right side when I cough or take a deep breath."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start? How long have you been sick?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been sick for about 4 days now. Started with body aches and chills, then developed this bad cough. Today the breathing got worse which is why I came in.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline helps differentiate viral prodrome with bacterial superinfection from other causes

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about what you're coughing up
historyQuestions.answer: It's thick and greenish-yellow. Sometimes there's quite a bit of it. No blood though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Purulent sputum suggests bacterial infection

historyQuestions.text: How high have your fevers been?
historyQuestions.answer: My wife checked last night and it was 102. I've been having chills and sweats too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High fever with rigors suggests bacterial infection

historyQuestions.text: Any chest pain, especially with breathing?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, actually. It hurts on my right side when I take a deep breath or cough.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pleuritic chest pain suggests pleural involvement - seen in pneumonia and PE

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any confusion or difficulty thinking clearly?
historyQuestions.answer: My wife says I've been a little out of it. I had to think about what day it was.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confusion is CURB-65 criterion and indicates severe disease

historyQuestions.text: How bad is your shortness of breath? What makes it worse?
historyQuestions.answer: It's probably a 6 out of 10. It's worse when I walk around or try to do anything. Even talking makes me a bit winded.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Severity and exertional component help gauge respiratory compromise

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just lisinopril for blood pressure and a baby aspirin. My wife gave me some Tylenol for the fever.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication review important for drug interactions and identifying immunosuppression

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Just high blood pressure. Been controlled for years. Never had pneumonia before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects severity assessment and treatment decisions

historyQuestions.text: Anyone around you been sick?
historyQuestions.answer: My wife had a cold last week, but she didn't get this bad.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Viral URI can precede bacterial superinfection

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, about half a pack a day for 25 years. I know I should quit.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking is major risk factor for pneumonia and affects follow-up planning

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: A couple beers on the weekend, nothing heavy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Heavy alcohol use is aspiration risk factor and affects pathogen considerations

historyQuestions.text: Have you been hospitalized recently or had any procedures?
historyQuestions.answer: No, haven't been in a hospital in years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent hospitalization changes classification to HCAP with different pathogen coverage

historyQuestions.text: Any choking episodes or trouble swallowing?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I swallow fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Aspiration risk factors suggest different pathogen coverage needed

historyQuestions.text: Any conditions affecting your immune system? Diabetes? HIV? Steroids?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that. Just the blood pressure.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Immunocompromise changes pathogen considerations

historyQuestions.text: Any recent travel?
historyQuestions.answer: No, haven't gone anywhere. Just been around home and work.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Travel history important for endemic fungi, Legionella exposure

historyQuestions.text: Any leg swelling or leg pain?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my legs are fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: DVT symptoms would raise suspicion for PE

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, penicillin gives me a rash. Nothing else that I know of.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical before prescribing antibiotics - penicillin allergy affects treatment choice

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: I had my appendix out when I was 25 and a knee scope for a torn meniscus about 10 years ago. Both went fine, no complications.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history important for anesthesia risk if intubation needed

historyQuestions.text: Any medical problems that run in your family?
historyQuestions.answer: My dad had a heart attack at 62, mom has diabetes. No lung problems in the family that I know of.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history helps assess cardiovascular risk

historyQuestions.text: Are you up to date on your vaccines? Flu shot? Pneumonia vaccine?
historyQuestions.answer: I got a flu shot last fall at work. I don't think I've ever had a pneumonia shot though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vaccination status affects prevention counseling and possibly etiology considerations

historyQuestions.text: Have you seen anyone else about this or been to the ER before coming here?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I thought it was just a cold at first. Finally came in when the breathing got bad today.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior workup and treatment affects current management

historyQuestions.text: When did you last see your regular doctor?
historyQuestions.answer: About 6 months ago for my blood pressure check. Everything was fine then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: PCP relationship important for follow-up planning

historyQuestions.text: What do you do for work?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm an electrician. Work on construction sites mostly. Been doing it for 30 years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Occupational exposures can affect pathogen considerations

historyQuestions.text: Who do you live with? Anyone at home who can help take care of you?
historyQuestions.answer: I live with my wife. She's been worried sick about me. We have a one-story house, no stairs to deal with.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Social support affects disposition - outpatient vs admission decision

historyQuestions.text: How has this been affecting your daily activities?
historyQuestions.answer: I haven't been able to work for 3 days. Can barely walk to the bathroom without getting winded. Sleeping has been terrible with all the coughing.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Functional impact helps gauge severity and recovery expectations

historyQuestions.text: What are you most worried about? What were you hoping we could do for you today?
historyQuestions.answer: Honestly, I'm worried it's something serious. My buddy got pneumonia last year and ended up in the ICU. I just want to be able to breathe normally again.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Understanding patient concerns helps with shared decision-making and expectation setting

physicalExam.reasoning: Fever, tachycardia, tachypnea, hypoxia - meets SIRS criteria. Calculate CURB-65.

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing male, flushed, mild respiratory distress. Speaking in full sentences but pausing to catch breath.
physicalExam.reasoning: General appearance helps gauge severity

physicalExam.finding: Crackles (rales) in right lower lobe posteriorly. Bronchial breath sounds over right base. Increased tactile fremitus on right. Left lung clear.
physicalExam.reasoning: Focal crackles, bronchial breath sounds, increased fremitus = consolidation

physicalExam.finding: Dullness to percussion at right base. Remainder of chest resonant.
physicalExam.reasoning: Dullness indicates consolidation or effusion

physicalExam.finding: Positive egophony ('E to A' change) over right lower lobe.
physicalExam.reasoning: Egophony indicates consolidation - sound transmission through solid lung

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs.
physicalExam.reasoning: Baseline cardiac exam

physicalExam.finding: Alert but slow to answer. Oriented to person and place, uncertain of exact date (says 'Thursday' when it's Friday).
physicalExam.reasoning: Confusion indicates severe pneumonia - adds to CURB-65 score

physicalExam.finding: No lower extremity edema or calf tenderness. Warm, well-perfused.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out DVT signs and heart failure

labs.name: Complete Blood Count
labs.result: WBC 18.4 with 88% neutrophils (left shift), Hgb 14.2, Plt 245
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis with left shift supports bacterial infection

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 136, K 4.0, Cl 100, CO2 22, BUN 24, Cr 1.1, Glucose 128
labs.reasoning: BUN >19 is CURB-65 criterion. Baseline renal function for antibiotic dosing.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Elevated lactate indicates tissue hypoperfusion - sepsis marker

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.reasoning: Per ATS/IDSA 2019: Procalcitonin can help distinguish bacterial from viral and guide antibiotic DISCONTINUATION. Serial procalcitonin trending down supports stopping antibiotics. However, initial antibiotic decision should be clinical, not delayed for procalcitonin.

labs.name: Blood Cultures x2
labs.reasoning: Must obtain before antibiotics in admitted patients. Positive in ~10% of CAP.

labs.name: Sputum Gram Stain and Culture
labs.result: Gram stain: many PMNs, gram-positive diplococci. Culture pending.
labs.reasoning: Gram-positive diplococci suggest S. pneumoniae

labs.name: Urine Legionella Antigen
labs.reasoning: Rules out Legionella, especially important if travel or outbreak

labs.name: Urine Pneumococcal Antigen
labs.reasoning: Confirms S. pneumoniae as causative organism

imaging.name: Chest X-ray (PA and Lateral)
imaging.result: Right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms. Small right-sided parapneumonic effusion. No pneumothorax. Heart size normal.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms pneumonia with consolidation. Small effusion is common.

imaging.name: CT Chest
imaging.result: Not routinely indicated for uncomplicated CAP
imaging.reasoning: Reserve for complicated cases, suspected abscess, or failure to improve

treatments.name: Supplemental oxygen
treatments.result: 2L NC applied, SpO2 improved to 95%
treatments.reasoning: Treat hypoxemia

treatments.name: IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 1L NS bolus given for elevated lactate
treatments.reasoning: Sepsis protocol - 30mL/kg crystalloid for hypoperfusion

treatments.name: Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin
treatments.result: Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily + Azithromycin 500mg IV daily. Duration: 5-7 days total. Transition to oral when improving.
treatments.reasoning: ATS/IDSA 2019: Inpatient non-ICU CAP = beta-lactam + macrolide OR respiratory FQ alone.

treatments.name: Levofloxacin (if beta-lactam allergy)
treatments.result: Levofloxacin 750mg IV/PO daily. Same 5-7 day duration.
treatments.reasoning: FQ monotherapy is alternative if beta-lactam allergy. Reserve FQ to preserve utility.

treatments.name: MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage
treatments.result: NOT indicated for this patient. No prior MRSA/Pseudomonas isolation, no recent hospitalization with IV antibiotics.
treatments.reasoning: KEY 2019 CHANGE: HCAP category ABANDONED. Only cover MRSA/Pseudomonas if: (1) prior respiratory isolation of these organisms, OR (2) recent hospitalization + parenteral antibiotics. If started empirically and cultures negative at 48h with clinical improvement → DE-ESCALATE.

treatments.name: Antipyretics
treatments.result: Acetaminophen 650mg PO q6h PRN for fever/comfort
treatments.reasoning: Symptomatic treatment

treatments.name: DVT prophylaxis
treatments.result: Enoxaparin 40mg SubQ daily
treatments.reasoning: Hospitalized medical patients need VTE prophylaxis

disposition.name: Admit to Medicine floor
disposition.result: Appropriate - CURB-65 = 2 (confusion, BUN>19)
disposition.reasoning: CURB-65 ≥2 suggests hospital admission. Hypoxia further supports.

disposition.name: Admit to ICU
disposition.result: Not required for this patient. SEVERE CAP CRITERIA: Major = vasopressor requirement OR mechanical ventilation (direct ICU). Minor criteria (≥3 = consider ICU): RR≥30, PaO2/FiO2≤250, multilobar infiltrates, confusion, BUN≥20, WBC<4000, platelets<100k, temp<36°C, hypotension requiring fluids.
disposition.reasoning: This patient has 2-3 minor criteria (confusion, BUN>20, tachypnea) but no major criteria. Floor appropriate but monitor closely. KEY: Late ICU transfer from ward has HIGHER mortality than direct ICU admission - don't delay if deteriorating.

disposition.name: Discharge with oral antibiotics
disposition.result: Not appropriate given severity
disposition.reasoning: Hypoxia, confusion, elevated lactate require admission

curb65.name: CURB-65 Score
curb65.description: Predicts mortality in community-acquired pneumonia to guide disposition

components.criterion: Confusion (new disorientation to person, place, or time)
Blood pressure: SBP < 90 or DBP ≤ 60 mmHg

thisPatient.nextStep: Admit to hospital ward, consider ICU if additional severe CAP criteria
thisPatient.clinicalAction: Admit for IV antibiotics, oxygen therapy, and close monitoring. Score of 2 = ~9% 30-day mortality.

high.action: Consider ICU admission, especially if score ≥4

psi.name: Pneumonia Severity Index (PSI/PORT Score)
psi.description: Alternative to CURB-65 - more complex but more validated
psi.note: PSI uses 20 variables including demographics, comorbidities, exam findings, and labs. Class I-III: outpatient. Class IV-V: inpatient.

thisPatient.clinicalAction: PSI confirms CURB-65 assessment - hospitalization indicated

psi.source: Fine MJ et al. N Engl J Med. 1997;336(4):243-250

actualDiagnosis.name: Community-Acquired Pneumonia (Streptococcus pneumoniae) with Sepsis
keyFindings: 4-day prodrome of fever, cough, purulent sputum
Focal exam findings: RLL crackles, dullness, egophony, bronchial breath sounds
CXR: RLL consolidation with small parapneumonic effusion
Meets sepsis criteria: infection + SIRS (fever, tachycardia, tachypnea)
Smoking history - risk factor for pneumonia
teachingPoints: CURB-65: Confusion, Urea>19 (BUN), RR≥30, BP<90/60, Age≥65. Score 0-1=outpatient, 2=admit, 3+=ICU consideration. PSI is alternative (more complex but more validated).

SEVERE CAP CRITERIA (ATS/IDSA): Major = vasopressors OR mechanical ventilation → direct ICU. Minor (≥3 = consider ICU): RR≥30, PaO2/FiO2≤250, multilobar, confusion, BUN≥20, WBC<4k, Plt<100k, temp<36°C, hypotension requiring fluids.
LATE ICU TRANSFER WARNING: Transfer from floor to ICU has HIGHER mortality than direct ICU admission. If ≥3 minor criteria, consider direct ICU.
TREATMENT DURATION: 5-7 days MINIMUM (not 10-14 days). Procalcitonin can guide discontinuation. Transition IV→PO when afebrile, improving, tolerating PO.

HCAP CATEGORY ABANDONED (2019): Do NOT use nursing home, dialysis, etc. to guide extended-spectrum coverage. Only cover MRSA/Pseudomonas if: prior respiratory isolation OR recent hospitalization + IV antibiotics.
DE-ESCALATION: If MRSA/Pseudomonas started empirically and cultures negative at 48h with improvement → de-escalate to standard CAP therapy.
MRSA NASAL PCR: Negative PCR has high negative predictive value - can withhold/stop MRSA coverage if negative.
Outpatient CAP: Healthy = amoxicillin OR doxycycline. Comorbidities = respiratory FQ OR beta-lactam + macrolide
Inpatient non-ICU: Respiratory FQ alone OR beta-lactam + macrolide. Inpatient ICU: Beta-lactam + (macrolide OR FQ) - NEVER FQ monotherapy for severe CAP.
Always obtain blood cultures before antibiotics in admitted patients. Positive in ~10% but guides therapy.
S. pneumoniae remains most common CAP pathogen. Atypicals (Mycoplasma, Legionella, Chlamydia) require macrolide or FQ coverage.
Smokers and age >50 need follow-up CXR in 6-8 weeks to ensure resolution and rule out underlying malignancy

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: CURB-65 score reliably stratifies mortality risk and guides disposition
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Score 0-1: outpatient (mortality <3%). Score 2: admit (mortality 9%). Score 3-5: consider ICU (mortality 15-40%)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: 5-day treatment is non-inferior to longer courses for CAP
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Uranga A et al., JAMA Intern Med 2016; el Moussaoui R et al., BMJ 2006
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Stop antibiotics at day 5 if afebrile x48h and clinically stable. Shorter courses reduce adverse effects and resistance
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Clinical success at day 10: 93% (5-day) vs 92% (standard). No difference in relapse or mortality

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Procalcitonin-guided discontinuation safely reduces antibiotic duration
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Schuetz P et al., Lancet Infect Dis 2018 (meta-analysis)
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Consider stopping antibiotics when PCT <0.25 μg/L or drops by ≥80% from peak
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Antibiotic exposure reduced 2.4 days. Mortality actually LOWER in PCT group (8.6% vs 10.0%)

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: Macrolide Mortality Benefit in Severe CAP
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Adding a macrolide to beta-lactam reduces mortality in severe CAP
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Sligl WI et al., Crit Care Med 2014 (meta-analysis)
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Always include macrolide (or fluoroquinolone) in severe CAP, not just for atypical coverage - immunomodulatory benefit
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Mortality reduction 18% (OR 0.82) with macrolide-containing regimen in severe CAP

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Corticosteroids reduce treatment failure and length of stay in severe CAP
evidenceBasedPearls.source: CAPE COD Trial, Dequin PF et al., NEJM 2023
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Consider hydrocortisone 200mg/day x4-7 days for severe CAP requiring ICU admission

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: MRSA Nasal PCR Negative Predictive Value
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Negative MRSA nasal PCR essentially rules out MRSA pneumonia
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Baby N et al., Clin Infect Dis 2009; Dangerfield B et al., Clin Infect Dis 2014
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: If MRSA nasal swab PCR is negative, can safely withhold/discontinue vancomycin/linezolid
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: NPV 95-99% for MRSA pneumonia. Can reduce unnecessary vancomycin use by ~50%

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Pneumococcal UAT allows early identification of S. pneumoniae even after antibiotics
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Johansson N et al., Clin Infect Dis 2010
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Positive UAT supports de-escalation to narrow-spectrum beta-lactam. Remains positive for weeks
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Sensitivity 80-90% for pneumococcal CAP. Allows pathogen-directed therapy

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Early mobilization reduces length of stay without increasing adverse events
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Get patients out of bed within 24 hours of admission when possible
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Length of stay reduced by 1.1 days (5.8 vs 6.9 days). No increase in complications

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Early IV-to-oral switch is safe when clinical stability criteria met
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Switch to oral when: afebrile, HR normalizing, tolerating PO, no unstable comorbidities
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: No difference in clinical cure (83% vs 85%). LOS reduced by ~2 days with early switch

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Azithromycin associated with small increased risk of cardiovascular death
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Use caution with azithromycin in patients with QT prolongation or cardiac risk factors
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: 2.88-fold increased risk of cardiovascular death during 5-day course. Absolute risk small but real

guidelineReferences.title: 2019 ATS/IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-Acquired Pneumonia
guidelineReferences.originalSource: Metlay JP, et al. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2019;200(7):e45-e67

guidelineReferences.title: 2016 ATS/IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines: Management of Adults with Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia
guidelineReferences.originalSource: Kalil AC, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2016;63(5):e61-e111

patientBackground.recentEvents: Marcus was feeling fine until about 5 days ago when he noticed a scratchy throat after his grandson visited with a cold. He continued working his usual shifts at the warehouse, pushing through increasing fatigue. Two days ago the cough became productive with yellow-green sputum, and yesterday he developed shaking chills that prompted his wife to insist he come to the hospital.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: Had what he thought was just a cold last week - runny nose, mild sore throat. It seemed to improve for a day, then came back worse with deeper cough and now this fever. He tried over-the-counter cold medicine which did not help.
patientBackground.fluidIntake: Has not been eating or drinking much for the past 2 days because nothing tastes right and he feels nauseous. Wife estimates maybe 2-3 glasses of water and some soup broth.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Works in a cold warehouse environment, often going in and out of refrigerated sections. Has been stressed about a big inventory deadline. Smoked for 20 years but quit 5 years ago. Recently his diabetes has been harder to control.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Lives with wife Denise of 32 years. Two adult children live within 30 minutes and are very involved. Wife is a retired nurse and is very concerned because "he never gets this sick."
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Marcus is generally stoic and does not like to complain. He resisted coming to the ER, saying "it's just a bad cold." His wife had to convince him this was different. He trusts doctors but does not like being in hospitals.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Last ER visit was 3 years ago for chest pain that turned out to be GERD. Has never been hospitalized for respiratory illness. Gets his flu shot every year at the pharmacy.
patientBackground.occupation: Warehouse supervisor for a food distribution company. On his feet all day, physical job. Has good health insurance through work.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was immunocompromised?
whatIfScenarios.description: The patient has been on prednisone 20mg daily for rheumatoid arthritis for 3 months

chiefComplaint.original: I've had a bad cough with fever for 4 days and now I'm having trouble breathing
chiefComplaint.modified: I've had a bad cough with fever for 4 days and now I'm having trouble breathing. I take prednisone for my rheumatoid arthritis.

feedback.correct: Excellent! Immunocompromised patients require broader differential including opportunistic infections. The blunted fever (steroids) is a key clue.
feedback.partial: Good start, but remember steroids blunt inflammatory response. Consider PCP (Pneumocystis), fungal, and atypical organisms.
feedback.incorrect: In immunocompromised hosts, typical CAP organisms may be less likely than opportunistic infections. Consider PCP, fungi, and mycobacteria.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: In immunocompetent patients, S. pneumoniae is most common. With chronic steroids, consider PCP (especially if CD4-equivalent low), Aspergillus, and atypical mycobacteria. May need bronchoscopy with BAL for diagnosis.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had aspiration risk factors?
whatIfScenarios.description: The patient has a history of stroke with mild dysphagia and was recently intoxicated

chiefComplaint.original: I've had a bad cough with fever for 4 days and now I'm having trouble breathing
chiefComplaint.modified: I've had a bad cough with fever for 4 days. I had a stroke last year and sometimes choke when I eat. I had a few too many drinks 5 days ago.

keyDiscriminators: Lower lobe or posterior segment location

feedback.correct: Excellent! Aspiration pneumonia requires coverage for oral anaerobes. Location is often dependent segments (posterior lower lobes, superior lower lobe).
feedback.partial: Consider the aspiration risk factors. Anaerobic coverage may be needed, and complications like abscess are more common.
feedback.incorrect: Dysphagia + alcohol intoxication = high aspiration risk. This changes antibiotic selection and prognosis significantly.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Unlike typical CAP, aspiration pneumonia often involves oral anaerobes and polymicrobial infection. May need anaerobic coverage (ampicillin-sulbactam, clindamycin) and higher risk of abscess/empyema. Location typically in dependent lung segments.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had normal oxygen saturation?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but SpO2 is 97% on room air

feedback.correct: Good recognition that normal SpO2 changes severity stratification. May still need admission if other CURB-65 criteria present, but outpatient treatment becomes consideration.
feedback.partial: Consider how normal oxygenation affects the severity score and disposition decision.
feedback.incorrect: Normal oxygen saturation significantly affects CAP severity. Recalculate CURB-65 and reassess need for admission.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Without hypoxia and with fewer severe CAP criteria, outpatient treatment may be appropriate if CURB-65 = 0-1. Key difference is disposition - not the diagnosis itself.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

female-24-trigger.name: Susan Mitchell
female-24-trigger.chiefComplaint: I can't catch my breath and my chest feels tight
female-24-trigger.variantDescription: Classic asthma exacerbation with identifiable trigger

missedConsequences.Asthma Exacerbation: Undertreated asthma leads to respiratory failure requiring intubation. Prolonged ICU stay with risk of hypoxic brain injury.
missedConsequences.Pneumothorax: Missed tension pneumothorax leads to cardiovascular collapse and cardiac arrest.
missedConsequences.Anaphylaxis: Unrecognized allergic reaction progresses to complete airway obstruction and death.

commonPitfalls: Relying solely on SpO2 - a 'normal' SpO2 in a tachypneic patient may indicate impending respiratory failure
Not recognizing 'silent chest' as an ominous sign of severe airway obstruction
Forgetting to ask about recent inhaler use and assess response to initial treatment
Underestimating severity based on patient's ability to speak in full sentences
Not considering pneumothorax in a patient with sudden worsening despite treatment

mcqs.question: The patient reports using her rescue inhaler 8 times in the past 24 hours with minimal relief. What does this indicate?

options.text: Normal rescue inhaler use during an exacerbation
options.explanation: More than 4-6 puffs/day indicates poor control and need for escalation.
Frequent rescue inhaler use (>4-6 times/day) with poor response indicates severe exacerbation needing systemic steroids and continuous bronchodilators.

options.text: Patient is overusing medication and should wait longer between doses
options.explanation: This is dangerous advice - the patient clearly needs more intensive therapy.

options.text: The inhaler is likely expired and should be replaced
options.explanation: While possible, the clinical picture indicates severe exacerbation regardless of inhaler efficacy.

mcqs.question: On exam, wheezing suddenly disappears while the patient remains tachypneic and distressed. What is the MOST concerning interpretation?

options.text: The bronchodilator is working and airway obstruction is improving
options.explanation: Disappearance of wheezing with worsening clinical status is ominous, not reassuring.

options.text: The patient is fatiguing and may have impending respiratory failure
options.explanation: 'Silent chest' occurs when airflow is so limited that wheezing cannot be generated. This indicates critical obstruction and impending respiratory failure.
Clinical correlation should come first - assess the patient's work of breathing and mental status.

options.text: The patient has developed pneumonia as a complication
options.explanation: Pneumonia doesn't cause sudden disappearance of wheezing; this is far more concerning.

allDiagnoses.name: Asthma Exacerbation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Known asthmatic with classic symptoms of wheezing, dyspnea, and chest tightness after URI trigger. Tachypnea and hypoxia indicate moderate-severe exacerbation.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Upper Respiratory Infection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cough and URI symptoms for 4 days are the likely trigger for the asthma exacerbation.

allDiagnoses.name: Bronchitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Productive cough with URI symptoms is consistent with acute bronchitis, which commonly triggers asthma.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumothorax
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can occur spontaneously or from severe coughing. Sudden worsening dyspnea in asthmatic must prompt consideration.

allDiagnoses.name: Anaphylaxis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present with wheezing and dyspnea. Always ask about new exposures, medications, foods.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Young women on OCPs have increased risk. Tachycardia and hypoxia can be seen in both PE and asthma.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Fever is low-grade and cough is not highly productive. No consolidation symptoms, but CXR should be considered.

allDiagnoses.name: Heart Failure
allDiagnoses.rationale: Rare in 24-year-old without cardiac history. No leg edema, orthopnea history, or JVD.

allDiagnoses.name: Vocal Cord Dysfunction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can mimic asthma but typically presents with inspiratory stridor, not expiratory wheezing.

allDiagnoses.name: Foreign Body Aspiration
allDiagnoses.rationale: No choking episode or sudden onset. More common in children and elderly.

female-24-trigger.oldcartsNarrative: "I've been having trouble breathing for about 2 days now, but it got really bad this morning. It started after I got this cold about 4 days ago. My chest feels really tight, like someone is squeezing it, and I keep coughing. I'd say the breathing difficulty is an 8 out of 10 right now. Sitting up and leaning forward helps a little, but lying down makes it way worse. My rescue inhaler usually works, but I've used it like 8 times since yesterday and it barely helps anymore. I have asthma - been diagnosed since I was a kid. I usually use my maintenance inhaler but I ran out last week and haven't picked up my refill. I've had bad attacks before but never been intubated. I take birth control pills and that's it. I don't smoke but my roommate does."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start? When did it get worse?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been having trouble breathing for about 2 days, but it got really bad this morning. It all started after I got this cold about 4 days ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline helps identify trigger (viral URI) and progression of exacerbation

historyQuestions.text: How often have you been using your rescue inhaler?
historyQuestions.answer: I've used it probably 8 or 9 times since yesterday. It helps for maybe 15-20 minutes then the tightness comes right back.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Frequent rescue inhaler use with poor response indicates severe exacerbation requiring systemic therapy

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm supposed to use a Flovent inhaler twice a day and I have an albuterol rescue inhaler. I also take birth control pills. I ran out of the Flovent about a week ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication review essential - identifies lapsed maintenance therapy and OCP use (PE risk factor)

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your regular asthma medications
historyQuestions.answer: I'm supposed to use a steroid inhaler twice a day - Flovent I think - but I ran out about a week ago and haven't had time to get a refill.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Lapsed maintenance therapy significantly increases exacerbation risk and severity

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever been hospitalized or intubated for asthma?
historyQuestions.answer: I was in the hospital once in high school, but I've never been on a breathing machine or in the ICU.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior hospitalizations and especially intubations indicate higher risk of severe/fatal asthma

historyQuestions.text: Are you having trouble speaking in full sentences?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah... I have to... take breaths... between words...
historyQuestions.reasoning: Inability to speak in full sentences indicates severe exacerbation

historyQuestions.text: What usually triggers your asthma?
historyQuestions.answer: Colds are the worst trigger. Also dust, cats, and sometimes strong perfumes. Cold air in winter too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Identifying triggers helps confirm diagnosis and guides future prevention

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I don't smoke. But my roommate smokes and sometimes it bothers me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking history important - secondhand smoke is asthma trigger

historyQuestions.text: Any new rashes, hives, swelling, or foods you've eaten?
historyQuestions.answer: No rashes or swelling. I haven't eaten anything unusual.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important to rule out anaphylaxis as cause of acute wheezing

historyQuestions.text: Any sharp chest pain, especially with deep breaths?
historyQuestions.answer: It's more of a tightness all over, not a sharp pain. Taking deep breaths is hard but doesn't cause sharp pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sharp pleuritic pain would increase concern for PTX or PE

historyQuestions.text: Any leg pain or swelling recently?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my legs are fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: DVT symptoms would increase PE suspicion, especially with OCP use

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any fevers?
historyQuestions.answer: I've felt a little warm with this cold but nothing high. Haven't really been checking.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High fever would raise concern for bacterial pneumonia complicating asthma

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies to medications.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical before giving any medications

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems besides asthma?
historyQuestions.answer: No, just the asthma. I've been pretty healthy otherwise.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms no comorbidities that would complicate management

historyQuestions.text: How severe is your asthma usually? Ever been hospitalized or intubated?
historyQuestions.answer: I've had some bad attacks before and been to the ER a few times, but I've never been in the ICU or intubated. Usually steroids and nebs fix me up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior near-fatal asthma (intubation) is major risk factor for future severe attacks

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I don't smoke. But my roommate smokes and I've been around that more since being home sick.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Secondhand smoke is a major asthma trigger

historyQuestions.text: What usually triggers your asthma?
historyQuestions.answer: Colds are the worst. Also cats, dust, and cigarette smoke. And exercise when it's cold out.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Identifying triggers helps with prevention counseling

historyQuestions.text: When did you last need steroids for your asthma?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe a year ago? I had a bad attack and they gave me prednisone for 5 days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Frequent steroid courses indicate poorly controlled asthma

historyQuestions.text: Do you have an asthma action plan?
historyQuestions.answer: I think I got one years ago but I don't really use it. I just use my inhaler when I need it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Action plan adherence is part of asthma education

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: BP 128/78, HR 112 and regular, RR 28, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 91% on room air
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia, tachypnea, and hypoxia indicate moderate-severe exacerbation

physicalExam.finding: Alert but anxious appearing, sitting upright leaning forward with hands on knees (tripod position). Speaking in 2-3 word phrases. Using accessory muscles. Appears fatigued.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tripod positioning and accessory muscle use indicate severe respiratory distress

physicalExam.finding: Increased work of breathing with visible supraclavicular, intercostal, and subcostal retractions. Nasal flaring present. Prolonged expiratory phase.
physicalExam.reasoning: Accessory muscle use and retractions indicate severe obstruction

physicalExam.finding: Diffuse bilateral expiratory wheezes in all lung fields. Decreased air movement throughout. Prolonged expiratory phase. No focal crackles.
physicalExam.reasoning: Diffuse expiratory wheezing is classic for asthma; decreased air movement concerning for severe obstruction

physicalExam.finding: PEF 180 L/min (personal best reported as 450 L/min) - approximately 40% of personal best.
physicalExam.reasoning: PEF <50% personal best indicates severe exacerbation

physicalExam.finding: Resonant percussion throughout. No hyperresonance or dullness.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hyperresonance would suggest pneumothorax; dullness would suggest consolidation

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs. Pulsus paradoxus measured at 18 mmHg.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg indicates severe airway obstruction

physicalExam.finding: No rashes, urticaria, or angioedema. No cyanosis. Skin slightly diaphoretic.
physicalExam.reasoning: Absence of urticaria/angioedema argues against anaphylaxis

labs.name: Arterial Blood Gas
labs.result: pH 7.42, pCO2 36, pO2 62, HCO3 24. A-a gradient elevated.
labs.reasoning: Normal or low pCO2 expected early (hyperventilation). Rising pCO2 indicates fatigue/impending failure.

labs.name: Complete Blood Count
labs.reasoning: Usually normal in uncomplicated asthma.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 140, K 3.8, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 14, Cr 0.9, Glucose 118
labs.reasoning: Baseline before beta-agonist therapy which can cause hypokalemia.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray (portable)
imaging.result: Hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms bilaterally. No focal consolidation. No pneumothorax. Heart size normal.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out pneumonia, pneumothorax, and pulmonary edema.

treatments.name: Supplemental oxygen
treatments.result: 2L NC applied, SpO2 improved to 95%
treatments.reasoning: Treat hypoxemia; target SpO2 92-96%

treatments.name: Continuous nebulized albuterol
treatments.result: 10-15 mg/hr continuous nebulization initiated
treatments.reasoning: First-line for severe exacerbation

treatments.name: Ipratropium bromide
treatments.result: 0.5 mg nebulized with first albuterol treatment
treatments.reasoning: Anticholinergic adds bronchodilation in severe exacerbations

treatments.name: Systemic corticosteroids
treatments.result: Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV given
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL - reduces inflammation. Should be given within first hour.

treatments.name: IV Magnesium sulfate
treatments.result: 2g IV over 20 minutes
treatments.reasoning: Consider in severe exacerbation not responding to initial therapy

disposition.name: Admit to ICU
disposition.result: Consider if not responding to treatment or impending failure
disposition.reasoning: ICU for impending failure, altered mental status, or need for continuous nebulizers

disposition.name: Admit to Medicine floor
disposition.result: Appropriate after initial stabilization
disposition.reasoning: Most moderate-severe exacerbations need admission

disposition.name: Discharge home
disposition.result: Not appropriate with current severity
disposition.reasoning: SpO2 91%, speaking in phrases, 40% PEF indicates need for admission

actualDiagnosis.name: Moderate-Severe Asthma Exacerbation (triggered by viral URI)
keyFindings: Frequent rescue inhaler use with poor response
Accessory muscle use and tripod positioning
teachingPoints: Severity assessment: speech (sentences vs phrases vs words), accessory muscle use, PEF, SpO2, mental status
Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg indicates severe obstruction
'Silent chest' in a dyspneic patient is OMINOUS - indicates minimal airflow
Systemic corticosteroids should be given EARLY (within first hour)
A 'normal' pCO2 in a tachypneic asthmatic is concerning - indicates tiring/failure
Discharge criteria: PEF >70%, SpO2 >94% on room air, speaking in full sentences

guidelineReferences.name: GINA Global Strategy for Asthma Management 2024
guidelineReferences.citation: Global Initiative for Asthma
keyPoints: Severity: speech, accessory muscles, PEF, SpO2, mental status
SABA + ipratropium for severe exacerbations
Systemic steroids within first hour of presentation
Discharge: PEF >60-80%, symptoms controlled, adequate inhaler technique
Step-up therapy for 2-4 weeks post-exacerbation

guidelineReferences.name: NAEPP Expert Panel Report 4: Asthma Management
guidelineReferences.citation: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute. 2020
keyPoints: High-dose SABA: first-line for acute exacerbation
Add ipratropium for severe exacerbations
Oral steroids equivalent to IV for most patients
Consider magnesium sulfate for severe exacerbations
Silent chest = impending respiratory failure

patientBackground.recentEvents: Brianna started a new job at an Amazon fulfillment center two weeks ago - much dustier than her old office job. Last week she adopted a kitten from the shelter, something she has wanted for years. Over the past 3 days her breathing has gotten progressively worse despite using her rescue inhaler more frequently.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: Had a mild upper respiratory infection about 10 days ago - runny nose and congestion that mostly resolved. Her asthma has been "okay" over the past year with only occasional rescue inhaler use, but she admits she has not been taking her maintenance inhaler consistently.
patientBackground.fluidIntake: Drinking normally but has not had much appetite due to difficulty breathing.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Just went through a stressful breakup last month. Moved to a new apartment that allows pets - older building, might have some dust issues. New job does not have great air quality but she needs the income. Cannot afford to miss work.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Lives alone now after the breakup. Parents live two hours away but are supportive by phone. Has a good friend nearby who drove her to the ER today. No kids.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Brianna has had asthma since childhood and considers herself an expert on managing it. She does not like taking daily medications and prefers to just use her rescue inhaler when needed. Tends to downplay symptoms until they get severe.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Has been to the ER twice before for asthma - once as a teenager (was intubated briefly), and once 3 years ago during a bad flare. That experience scared her, but she still struggles with medication adherence.
patientBackground.occupation: Just started at Amazon fulfillment center. Previously worked in an office. No health insurance yet - still in waiting period at new job.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the chest was silent?
whatIfScenarios.description: Severe respiratory distress but no wheezing audible on exam

chiefComplaint.original: I can't breathe - my inhaler isn't helping
chiefComplaint.modified: I can't breathe [patient is tripoding, speaking single words, appearing exhausted]

feedback.correct: SILENT CHEST = OMINOUS. No air movement = critical obstruction. Prepare for intubation. Ketamine is bronchodilator + sedative of choice for RSI in asthma.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of danger. Why is the silent chest concerning?
feedback.incorrect: Silent chest in a dyspneic asthmatic is NOT improvement - it means NO AIR MOVEMENT (severe obstruction). Combined with decreased RR and exhaustion = impending arrest. Prepare for intubation immediately.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Wheezing = air moving (some obstruction but not complete). Silent chest = minimal/no air moving (critical obstruction). Silence is worse than loud wheezing.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if pCO2 was 42 on ABG?
whatIfScenarios.description: Tachypneic, distressed asthmatic with 'normal' pCO2 of 42

chiefComplaint.original: I can't breathe - my inhaler isn't helping
chiefComplaint.modified: I can't breathe [patient is tachypneic at RR 32, working hard]

feedback.correct: A 'NORMAL' pCO2 in a tachypneic asthmatic is ABNORMAL. They should be hyperventilating (low CO2). Normal CO2 means they're tiring and can't maintain minute ventilation. ICU, prepare for intubation.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of abnormal 'normal.' Why should pCO2 be low?
feedback.incorrect: Tachypneic asthmatics should have LOW pCO2 (hyperventilating to compensate). 'Normal' pCO2 means muscles are fatiguing, can't blow off CO2. Rising CO2 is next. This patient needs ICU, possible intubation.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Expected ABG in asthma exacerbation: respiratory alkalosis (low CO2, high pH). Normal or high CO2 = failure/fatigue. Don't be reassured by 'normal' numbers.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was urticaria and throat swelling?
whatIfScenarios.description: Wheezing with hives, lip swelling, and throat tightness after bee sting

chiefComplaint.original: I can't breathe - my inhaler isn't helping
chiefComplaint.modified: I can't breathe after a bee sting - my lips are swelling and I'm itchy all over

feedback.correct: This is ANAPHYLAXIS, not just asthma. First-line treatment: IM EPINEPHRINE (0.3-0.5mg IM lateral thigh). Then albuterol, steroids, antihistamines. Prepare for airway intervention.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of anaphylaxis. What's the first-line treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Wheezing + urticaria + angioedema + allergen exposure = ANAPHYLAXIS. Treatment is EPINEPHRINE IM first (not just albuterol/steroids). 0.3-0.5mg IM to lateral thigh. Can repeat q5-15 min. Don't delay for other treatments.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Asthma = lower airway obstruction, wheezing. Anaphylaxis = multi-system (skin + respiratory + often cardiovascular), needs EPINEPHRINE as first-line. Don't just treat with asthma meds.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

male-67-smoker.name: Henry Johnson
male-67-smoker.chiefComplaint: My breathing's been getting worse for 3 days and I'm coughing up more junk than usual

missedConsequences.COPD Exacerbation: Untreated exacerbation leads to respiratory failure. CO2 narcosis from uncontrolled oxygen causes respiratory arrest.
missedConsequences.Pneumonia: Unrecognized pneumonia progresses to sepsis, ARDS, and multiorgan failure.
missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: Missed PE leads to right heart failure and death.
missedConsequences.Acute Coronary Syndrome: MI presents atypically in COPD patient; untreated leads to cardiogenic shock.

commonPitfalls: Giving high-flow oxygen and causing CO2 retention - COPD patients rely on hypoxic drive
Missing concurrent pneumonia by not getting a chest X-ray
Forgetting that PE risk is elevated in COPD exacerbation
Not checking an ABG/VBG to assess for hypercapnic respiratory failure
Withholding steroids due to concern for hyperglycemia - they are essential

mcqs.question: The patient has baseline home oxygen at 2L NC. His current SpO2 is 88%. What is the most appropriate oxygen management?

options.text: High-flow oxygen at 15 L/min via non-rebreather
options.explanation: Dangerous - high-flow O2 can suppress hypoxic drive and worsen CO2 retention in COPD.

options.text: Increase to 3-4 L NC and titrate to SpO2 88-92%
options.explanation: Correct - COPD patients rely on hypoxic drive. Target SpO2 88-92% to avoid CO2 retention.

options.text: Maintain at 2L NC since this is his baseline
options.explanation: Patient is hypoxic at 88% - needs increase, but carefully titrated.

options.text: Start BiPAP immediately before any supplemental oxygen
options.explanation: Supplemental O2 is appropriate first step. BiPAP reserved if not improving.

mcqs.question: ABG shows pH 7.32, pCO2 58, pO2 64, HCO3 30. How do you interpret this?

options.text: Acute respiratory acidosis - needs immediate intubation
options.explanation: Elevated HCO3 indicates chronic compensation, not purely acute.

options.text: Chronic respiratory acidosis with acute decompensation
options.explanation: Elevated HCO3 (30) indicates chronic CO2 retention with renal compensation. Acidemia (pH 7.32) indicates acute worsening - 'acute-on-chronic' respiratory failure.
This is respiratory acidosis (high pCO2), not metabolic.

options.text: Normal ABG for a COPD patient
options.explanation: pH 7.32 is acidemic and indicates decompensation requiring treatment.

allDiagnoses.name: COPD Exacerbation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic presentation - increased dyspnea, increased sputum production and purulence (Anthonisen criteria) in known COPD patient.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Bronchitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Often the trigger for COPD exacerbation. Increased cough with change in sputum character.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Upper Respiratory Infection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Common trigger for COPD exacerbation. Low-grade fever consistent with viral etiology.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Fever, productive cough, and hypoxia could indicate CAP. Must get chest X-ray.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: COPD patients have increased PE risk. Hypoxia out of proportion to exam raises suspicion.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Coronary Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present atypically in elderly/COPD patients. Check ECG and troponin.

allDiagnoses.name: Heart Failure Exacerbation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can coexist with COPD and present similarly. Look for JVD, edema, elevated BNP.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumothorax
allDiagnoses.rationale: COPD patients with bullous disease at risk. Sudden worsening should prompt consideration.

allDiagnoses.name: Lung Cancer
allDiagnoses.rationale: Heavy smoking history is risk factor. Consider if hemoptysis or failure to improve.

allDiagnoses.name: Bronchiectasis Exacerbation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can coexist with COPD. Usually more copious purulent sputum.

male-67-smoker.oldcartsNarrative: "My breathing's been getting worse for about 3 days now. It started with a cold - runny nose and scratchy throat - then moved into my chest. Now I'm coughing up a lot more phlegm than usual and it's turned greenish-yellow instead of my usual white. The shortness of breath is probably a 7 out of 10 - worse than my usual baseline of maybe 3 or 4. Walking to the bathroom winds me completely. Sitting upright helps. My rescue inhaler helps a little but not like it usually does. I've had COPD for about 10 years - I used to smoke two packs a day for 40 years but quit 5 years ago when I needed oxygen at home. I use my oxygen at 2 liters all the time now. I take Spiriva, Advair, and albuterol for rescue. I also take lisinopril and aspirin - had a heart attack about 3 years ago."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start getting worse?
historyQuestions.answer: About 3 days ago. It started with cold symptoms - runny nose and scratchy throat - then moved into my chest. The breathing has gotten progressively worse since then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline helps identify viral trigger and assess progression

historyQuestions.text: How has your sputum changed?
historyQuestions.answer: It's definitely more than usual - I'm filling up tissues all day. And the color changed from white to greenish-yellow about 2 days ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Increased sputum volume and purulence are Anthonisen criteria for COPD exacerbation

historyQuestions.text: What's your breathing like on a normal day?
historyQuestions.answer: Usually I can walk around the house okay with my oxygen. I get short of breath going up stairs. Now I can barely make it to the bathroom.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Understanding baseline helps assess severity of current exacerbation

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: I take Spiriva and Advair for my COPD, and albuterol as rescue. Also lisinopril and aspirin since my heart attack. I use oxygen at 2 liters all the time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication review identifies COPD severity (home O2) and cardiac history

historyQuestions.text: Have you been taking all your medications regularly?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I've been good about my inhalers. Haven't missed any doses. They're just not working as well right now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Non-compliance is common cause of exacerbation; confirms this is true worsening

historyQuestions.text: Are you still smoking?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I quit 5 years ago when I started needing oxygen. Smoked two packs a day for 40 years before that. Hardest thing I ever did.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Continued smoking vs. cessation affects prognosis and severity

historyQuestions.text: How many times have you been hospitalized for your COPD?
historyQuestions.answer: Probably 4 or 5 times over the years. Twice in the last year. I was in the ICU once but never on a ventilator.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Frequent exacerbations and prior ICU admission indicate high-risk patient

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your heart history
historyQuestions.answer: I had a heart attack about 3 years ago. They put in a stent. Been on aspirin and lisinopril since then. No problems with my heart lately.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cardiac history crucial - ACS and HF must be considered as alternative diagnoses

historyQuestions.text: Any chest pain or pressure?
historyQuestions.answer: No chest pain like my heart attack. Just the usual tightness from trying to breathe.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important to differentiate from ACS given cardiac history

historyQuestions.text: Any swelling in your legs?
historyQuestions.answer: They've always been a little puffy but maybe slightly worse the last couple days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: New edema may suggest cor pulmonale, right heart failure, or DVT

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any fevers?
historyQuestions.answer: I've felt a bit warm but haven't checked. Maybe a low-grade fever.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High fever increases concern for bacterial pneumonia

historyQuestions.text: Any blood in your sputum?
historyQuestions.answer: No blood, just the green stuff.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hemoptysis would raise concern for malignancy

historyQuestions.text: Any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm allergic to sulfa - got a bad rash from it years ago. No other allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before prescribing antibiotics; sulfa allergy affects some antibiotic choices

historyQuestions.text: Besides your COPD and heart history, any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure, which they control with the lisinopril. Some mild diabetes controlled with diet. That's about it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diabetes may worsen with steroid treatment; complete comorbidity assessment

historyQuestions.text: Any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: Just the heart catheterization and stent 3 years ago. Never had lung surgery.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No prior thoracic surgery

historyQuestions.text: Any lung problems in your family?
historyQuestions.answer: My father had emphysema too - he was a smoker. Died of it actually. No alpha-1 or anything like that though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of COPD; alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency would be important if positive

historyQuestions.text: Do you drink alcohol?
historyQuestions.answer: Not anymore. Had a few beers back in my smoking days but quit both when I got sick.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Alcohol history for complete social history

historyQuestions.text: Who do you live with?
historyQuestions.answer: I live with my wife. She helps me manage my oxygen and medications. She drove me in today.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Caregiver support important for COPD management

historyQuestions.text: Are you up to date on your flu shot and pneumonia vaccines?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I get my flu shot every fall. Had the pneumonia shot a couple years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vaccination status affects infection risk

historyQuestions.text: When did you last see your lung doctor?
historyQuestions.answer: About 3 months ago. He said my COPD was stable at that point. I have a pulmonary function test scheduled for next month.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent baseline assessment available; planned follow-up indicates engaged patient

historyQuestions.text: What are you most worried about?
historyQuestions.answer: I hate these flare-ups. Every time I worry it's going to be the one that puts me on a ventilator. I've seen other people with COPD go through that and it scares me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient has insight into disease severity and goals of care

physicalExam.finding: BP 148/92, HR 102 regular, RR 24, Temp 100.8°F, SpO2 88% on 2L NC (home baseline)
physicalExam.reasoning: Low-grade fever, tachycardia, tachypnea, and hypoxia indicate moderate-severe exacerbation

physicalExam.finding: Elderly male in moderate respiratory distress. Speaking in short sentences. Pursed-lip breathing. Leaning forward. Barrel-chested.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pursed-lip breathing and barrel chest are classic COPD findings

physicalExam.finding: Decreased breath sounds bilaterally, diffuse expiratory wheezes and scattered rhonchi. Prolonged expiratory phase. No focal crackles.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic COPD exam. Focal crackles would suggest pneumonia.

physicalExam.finding: Using sternocleidomastoid and scalene muscles. Intercostal retractions present. No paradoxical abdominal movement.
physicalExam.reasoning: Accessory muscle use indicates increased work of breathing

physicalExam.finding: Hyperresonant to percussion bilaterally. No dullness.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hyperresonance indicates air trapping; dullness would suggest consolidation

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Distant heart sounds. No murmurs or gallops.
physicalExam.reasoning: Distant heart sounds common in COPD due to hyperinflation

physicalExam.finding: JVP mildly elevated, approximately 9 cm H2O.
physicalExam.reasoning: Elevated JVP may indicate cor pulmonale or right heart failure

physicalExam.finding: Bilateral 1+ pitting edema to mid-calf, symmetric. No calf tenderness or asymmetry.
physicalExam.reasoning: Symmetric edema suggests cor pulmonale/CHF; asymmetry would increase DVT concern

physicalExam.finding: Alert but fatigued. Oriented to person, place, and time. No asterixis.
physicalExam.reasoning: Confusion or asterixis would indicate CO2 narcosis

labs.name: Arterial Blood Gas
labs.reasoning: Shows acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis. Elevated HCO3 indicates chronic compensation.

labs.name: Complete Blood Count
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis suggests infection. Polycythemia indicates chronic hypoxemia.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 138, K 4.2, Cl 98, CO2 30, BUN 24, Cr 1.2, Glucose 142
labs.reasoning: Elevated bicarbonate confirms chronic CO2 retention.

labs.name: BNP
labs.reasoning: Mild elevation can be seen in cor pulmonale. Very high levels would suggest heart failure.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.reasoning: Rules out acute MI given history of CAD

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.reasoning: Suggests possible bacterial infection, supports antibiotic use

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms. No focal consolidation. No pneumothorax. No effusions.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out pneumonia, pneumothorax, and pulmonary edema.

imaging.name: ECG
imaging.result: Sinus tachycardia at 102. Right axis deviation. P-pulmonale. No ST changes.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out acute MI. P-pulmonale consistent with chronic cor pulmonale.

treatments.name: Controlled oxygen therapy
treatments.result: Increased to 3L NC, SpO2 improved to 91%
treatments.reasoning: Target SpO2 88-92% to avoid suppressing hypoxic drive

treatments.name: Nebulized albuterol/ipratropium (DuoNeb)
treatments.result: 2.5mg/0.5mg nebulizer given, repeat in 1 hour
treatments.reasoning: First-line bronchodilator therapy for COPD exacerbation

treatments.name: Systemic corticosteroids
treatments.result: Prednisone 40mg PO (or methylprednisolone 125mg IV)
treatments.reasoning: ESSENTIAL - reduces inflammation and shortens exacerbation duration

treatments.name: Antibiotics
treatments.result: Azithromycin 500mg PO or Levofloxacin 750mg (penicillin allergy)
treatments.reasoning: Indicated for Anthonisen Type I exacerbation or purulent sputum

treatments.name: BiPAP (if needed)
treatments.result: Standing by - will initiate if pH <7.30 or worsening despite treatment
treatments.reasoning: NIPPV reduces intubation and mortality in hypercapnic respiratory failure

treatments.name: DVT prophylaxis
treatments.result: Enoxaparin 40mg SubQ daily
treatments.reasoning: Hospitalized medical patients need VTE prophylaxis

disposition.name: Admit to ICU
disposition.result: Consider if requiring BiPAP or high risk for intubation
disposition.reasoning: ICU for severe acidemia, impending failure, or hemodynamic instability

disposition.name: Admit to Medicine floor
disposition.result: Appropriate for this patient
disposition.reasoning: Moderate exacerbation with hypoxia, fever, and acidemia warrants admission

disposition.name: Discharge home
disposition.result: Not appropriate
disposition.reasoning: Hypoxemic, febrile, with respiratory acidosis - requires admission

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute COPD Exacerbation (Anthonisen Type I) with Acute-on-Chronic Hypercapnic Respiratory Failure
keyFindings: All 3 Anthonisen criteria: increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume, increased sputum purulence
Known severe COPD on home O2
ABG showing acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis (pH 7.32, pCO2 58, HCO3 30)
teachingPoints: Anthonisen criteria: increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume, increased sputum purulence. Type I = all 3; Type II = 2; Type III = 1
Target SpO2 88-92% in COPD to avoid suppressing hypoxic drive
ABG interpretation: chronic elevated HCO3 = compensation; acute pH drop = decompensation
BiPAP reduces intubation and mortality in acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH <7.35, pCO2 >45)
Steroids are ESSENTIAL - reduce treatment failure and shorten recovery
Antibiotics indicated for severe exacerbations (all 3 Anthonisen criteria) or purulent sputum
Always consider pneumonia, PE, ACS, and CHF in COPD exacerbation

guidelineReferences.name: GOLD Report: Global Strategy for COPD 2024
guidelineReferences.citation: Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease
keyPoints: Anthonisen criteria classify exacerbation severity
Target SpO2 88-92% to avoid hypercapnia
SABA + SAMA bronchodilators first-line
Systemic steroids (prednisone 40mg x 5 days)
Antibiotics for Type I or purulent sputum

guidelineReferences.name: ATS/ERS Guidelines on COPD Exacerbation Management
guidelineReferences.citation: Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2017;195(9):1253-1263
keyPoints: NIPPV for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure
Short-course steroids (5 days) as effective as longer
Procalcitonin can guide antibiotic decisions
Early mobilization reduces hospital stay
Reassess inhaler technique before discharge

patientBackground.recentEvents: Harold ran out of his Spiriva and Advair inhalers 5 days ago and has not been able to get to the pharmacy to refill them. His wife usually handles this but she has been in the hospital herself for a hip replacement. He has been more sedentary than usual, mostly sitting in his recliner watching TV.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: Baseline severe COPD - usually uses 2L oxygen at night and with exertion. Had bronchitis about 2 months ago that took a 10-day course of antibiotics and prednisone to clear. Since then he has not felt quite back to baseline.
patientBackground.fluidIntake: Has been drinking less because it is hard to get up to go to the bathroom. Maybe 3-4 glasses of water a day.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Fixed income from Social Security. Sometimes has to choose between medications and groceries. Lives in an older home - daughter worries about the air quality. Quit smoking 8 years ago after 45 pack-years. Still has significant guilt about his smoking history.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Wife is in rehab facility after hip surgery - she usually manages everything at home. Daughter Sandra lives 20 minutes away and has been trying to check in daily but works full time. Neighbor brings occasional meals.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Harold feels his COPD is his own fault from smoking. He is somewhat fatalistic about his health - "I made my bed." He follows doctor recommendations when he can afford the medications but does not advocate for himself well.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Multiple ER visits and hospitalizations for COPD over the past 5 years. Last admission was 6 months ago for a similar exacerbation. Has had discussions about goals of care but wants to continue aggressive treatment.
patientBackground.occupation: Retired pipe fitter. Worked with asbestos early in career before regulations. Has filed for asbestos-related benefits but paperwork is still pending.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if pH was 7.22 with altered mental status?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but ABG shows pH 7.22, pCO2 72, and patient is drowsy

chiefComplaint.original: Can't catch my breath - worse than usual
chiefComplaint.modified: Can't breathe... so tired... [patient is drowsy and confused]

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Severe COPD exacerbation with impending respiratory failure

feedback.correct: Severe acidosis + altered mental status + decreased RR = respiratory failure. BiPAP may still be tried but have low threshold for intubation. This patient needs ICU.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of severity. What's concerning about the respiratory rate being LOW?
feedback.incorrect: This is impending respiratory arrest. Decreased RR + drowsiness = CO2 narcosis, NOT improvement. BiPAP may work but prepare for intubation. ICU admission mandatory.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Moderate exacerbation (pH 7.32) = BiPAP often effective, floor admission possible. Severe (pH <7.25, altered, fatigue) = high risk of failure, ICU mandatory, intubation standby.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had chest pain?
whatIfScenarios.description: COPD exacerbation but with new substernal chest pressure

chiefComplaint.original: Can't catch my breath - worse than usual
chiefComplaint.modified: Can't breathe and having chest pressure

feedback.correct: Chest pain in COPD exacerbation = consider ACS (COPD is CAD risk factor) and PE (hypercoagulable in acute illness). Get ECG and troponin immediately.
feedback.partial: Good concern for cardiac involvement. What two diagnoses must be ruled out?
feedback.incorrect: COPD exacerbation + chest pain = must rule out ACS and PE. COPD patients have high CAD prevalence. Hypoxia can cause demand ischemia (Type 2 MI). Always get ECG and troponin.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Isolated COPD exacerbation = treat with standard therapy. COPD + chest pain = add cardiac workup (ECG, troponin, consider CTPA if PE concern). Type 2 MI common in COPD exacerbations.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had new leg swelling?
whatIfScenarios.description: COPD exacerbation with bilateral leg edema and elevated JVP

chiefComplaint.original: Can't catch my breath - worse than usual
chiefComplaint.modified: Can't breathe and my legs are really swollen

feedback.correct: Bilateral edema + JVD in COPD = cor pulmonale (right heart failure from pulmonary hypertension). May also have left heart component. Consider echo.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of right heart signs. What causes this in COPD patients?
feedback.incorrect: COPD leads to pulmonary hypertension which causes right heart failure (cor pulmonale). Signs: JVD, peripheral edema, hepatomegaly, RV heave. Need echo. May need diuretics in addition to COPD treatment.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Isolated COPD exacerbation = bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics. COPD + right heart failure = add diuretics, consider echo, may need different disposition and monitoring.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

guidelineReferences.name: ATS Dyspnea Evaluation Statement 2012
guidelineReferences.citation: Parshall MB, et al. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2012;185:435-452
keyPoints: Systematic evaluation for cause
Consider PE, pneumonia, HF, COPD
Pulse oximetry and ABG as indicated

female-32-asthma.name: J. Rivera
female-32-asthma.variantDescription: Asthma exacerbation with PE risk factors to rule out

missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: PE assumed to be 'just asthma' because patient has asthma history. Anticoagulation not initiated. Patient develops saddle PE and cardiac arrest 6 hours later.
missedConsequences.Asthma Exacerbation: If undertreated, patient develops respiratory failure. 'Silent chest' develops as airflow decreases - absence of wheezing is an ominous sign, not improvement.
missedConsequences.Pneumothorax: Missed pneumothorax worsens with positive pressure ventilation during asthma treatment. Tension physiology develops.

commonPitfalls: Assuming 'wheezing = asthma' without considering PE - PE can cause wheezing too!
Missing the VTE risk factors: 12-hour drive + oral contraceptives = high risk
Interpreting a 'quiet chest' as improvement - decreased breath sounds with severe dyspnea is a sign of impending respiratory failure
Not getting a D-dimer or CT-PA because 'she's young with known asthma'
Forgetting that asthma and PE can coexist - having one doesn't protect against the other
Undertreating: steroids are essential for all but the mildest exacerbations

mcqs.question: The patient has known asthma AND VTE risk factors (long drive + OCPs). Her symptoms could be from either. Which is the best initial approach?

options.text: Treat as asthma exacerbation; only consider PE if she doesn't improve
options.explanation: Dangerous delay. If this is PE, waiting to evaluate could be fatal.

options.text: Ignore the asthma and workup only for PE
