male-55-hottub.name: Robert Nelson
male-55-hottub.chiefComplaint: I keep getting this cough and shortness of breath - the doctors say it's pneumonia but it keeps coming back

missedConsequences.Hot Tub Lung: Continued exposure leads to chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis with irreversible pulmonary fibrosis.

commonPitfalls: Repeatedly treating as community-acquired pneumonia
Not asking about hot tub use
Missing the pattern of improvement when away from home
Not recognizing that NTM can cause HP not just infection

allDiagnoses.name: Hot Tub Lung (NTM HP)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Regular hot tub use + recurrent 'pneumonia' that clears on vacation + returns when home = hot tub lung.

allDiagnoses.name: NTM Pulmonary Infection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Could be infection rather than HP - sputum cultures will differentiate.

allDiagnoses.name: Recurrent Bacterial Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would not improve specifically when on vacation and return with hot tub use.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (other)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Hot tub is the clear exposure source.

male-55-hottub.oldcartsNarrative: "This is the fourth time in two years I've had this same thing - cough, shortness of breath, low-grade fevers, feeling lousy. The doctors keep calling it pneumonia and giving me antibiotics. They work eventually, but then it comes back a few months later. The weird thing is, when we went on a cruise last month, I felt great. But within a week of getting home, I was sick again. We installed a hot tub about two years ago and I use it almost every day."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the pattern of your symptoms
historyQuestions.answer: Sick every few months. Gets better, comes back. Always the same - cough, SOB, mild fever.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recurrent episodes over 2 years - same period as hot tub ownership

historyQuestions.text: You felt better on the cruise?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! I felt great the whole two weeks we were gone. Best I've felt in years. Then got sick again after we got home.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Symptoms resolve AWAY from home = environmental cause

historyQuestions.text: When did this pattern start?
historyQuestions.answer: About two years ago. We got a hot tub as a retirement gift to ourselves around that time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Symptoms started when hot tub was installed - direct temporal relationship

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your hot tub use
historyQuestions.answer: I love it. Use it almost every evening. It's an indoor hot tub in our sunroom.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Daily use + INDOOR location = maximum aerosol exposure

historyQuestions.text: How do you maintain the hot tub?
historyQuestions.answer: I check the chemicals weekly. It does get foamy sometimes. Probably should clean it more often.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Inadequate maintenance allows NTM biofilm to develop

historyQuestions.text: Does your wife have any symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: She doesn't use it as much as me, but she has had some coughing too lately.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Wife also affected - less exposure, milder symptoms

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Metformin for diabetes, blood pressure medication, and a baby aspirin.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication review; diabetes may affect lung healing

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before starting steroids or antibiotics

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure. Pretty well controlled.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diabetes may affect treatment response and infection risk

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: Quit 20 years ago. Smoked a pack a day for maybe 15 years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking history relevant for lung disease differential

historyQuestions.text: Any immune problems or immunosuppressive medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that. Just the diabetes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Immunosuppression would change NTM disease significance

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

hobbies_general.answer: I use our hot tub every day. It's my retirement treat. Indoor hot tub in our enclosed sunroom.
hobbies_general.relevance: Daily hot tub use with aerosolized water = massive exposure to NTM. INDOOR hot tubs are worse because aerosols concentrate in enclosed space.

housing_ventilation.answer: The sunroom where the hot tub is doesn't have great ventilation. Gets steamy.
housing_ventilation.relevance: Poor ventilation concentrates aerosolized NTM. Warm, humid enclosed space is perfect for NTM growth.

water_food_water_quality.answer: The hot tub water gets foamy sometimes. I try to keep up with chemicals but it's a lot of work.
water_food_water_quality.relevance: Foam indicates biofilm development. NTM form biofilms resistant to standard chlorine levels.

social_household_others_sick.answer: My wife has been coughing too, but not as bad as me. She doesn't use the tub as often.
social_household_others_sick.relevance: Dose-response relationship: less exposure = milder symptoms. Confirms hot tub is source.

housing_mold_visible.answer: There is some mold around the tub area. Hard to keep dry.
housing_mold_visible.relevance: Mold can also cause HP but NTM from hot tub is more likely cause here.

physicalExam.finding: Mildly ill-appearing. Speaks in short sentences due to dyspnea.
physicalExam.reasoning: Respiratory distress indicates significant lung involvement

physicalExam.finding: BP 132/78, HR 88, RR 20, Temp 100.2°F, SpO2 93% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Hypoxia and low-grade fever

physicalExam.finding: Diffuse fine inspiratory crackles throughout both lung fields
physicalExam.reasoning: Crackles indicate interstitial lung disease

physicalExam.finding: No clubbing yet
physicalExam.reasoning: No clubbing suggests not yet chronic fibrotic stage

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Mild leukocytosis

labs.name: Sputum AFB smear and culture
labs.result: AFB smear positive. Culture pending (M. avium complex suspected).
labs.reasoning: Identifies the NTM species. MAC is most common in hot tub lung.

labs.name: NTM PCR if available
labs.reasoning: Rapid identification of NTM

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Bilateral interstitial infiltrates
imaging.reasoning: Shows parenchymal disease

imaging.name: HRCT Chest
imaging.result: Diffuse ground-glass opacities with centrilobular nodules. Mosaic attenuation. Findings consistent with hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
imaging.reasoning: HRCT shows classic HP pattern - same as bird fancier's lung

procedures.name: Pulmonary function tests
procedures.result: FVC 68% predicted, FEV1 72%, FEV1/FVC 82% (normal ratio), DLCO 58% predicted. Restrictive pattern with impaired gas exchange.
procedures.reasoning: Confirms restrictive lung disease pattern consistent with HP. DLCO reduction indicates parenchymal damage.

procedures.name: Bronchoscopy with BAL (if diagnosis uncertain)
procedures.result: BAL shows lymphocytosis (45%) consistent with HP. AFB smear positive. Culture growing MAC.
procedures.reasoning: BAL lymphocytosis supports HP. Can also culture NTM from BAL if sputum negative.

treatments.name: HOT TUB REMOVAL (CRITICAL - DEFINITIVE TREATMENT)
treatments.result: CRITICAL: Hot tub must be drained and removed. Cleaning is NOT sufficient - NTM biofilms are resistant to chlorine. This is not negotiable for recovery.
treatments.reasoning: Removal of exposure is THE treatment. Unlike some HP where antigen avoidance is difficult, here it's clear: get rid of the hot tub. Continued exposure leads to irreversible fibrosis.

treatments.name: Prednisone 0.5-1mg/kg/day
treatments.result: Prednisone 40mg daily started for active HP. Will taper over 4-8 weeks once symptoms improve.
treatments.reasoning: Corticosteroids accelerate recovery in HP. Taper once symptoms and imaging improve. May need several weeks of treatment.

treatments.name: Supplemental oxygen
treatments.result: 2L NC for SpO2 <92%. Home oxygen arranged PRN for exertion.
treatments.reasoning: Supplemental oxygen for hypoxemia. May need during exertion even if resting SpO2 acceptable.

treatments.name: NTM antibiotics (if true infection, not just HP)
treatments.result: Defer NTM treatment - this is primarily HP reaction, not NTM infection. Would treat NTM if: cavitary disease, high organism burden, progressive despite exposure removal.
treatments.reasoning: Hot tub lung is usually HP (immune reaction) not NTM pulmonary infection. Don't treat MAC unless meeting ATS criteria for NTM pulmonary disease.

treatments.name: PPI for steroid GI protection
treatments.result: Omeprazole 20mg daily while on prednisone.
treatments.reasoning: GI prophylaxis during steroid therapy.

consults.name: Pulmonology consult
consults.result: Confirms hot tub lung. Emphatic that hot tub must be removed - not just cleaned. Will manage steroid taper and follow PFTs.
consults.reasoning: Pulmonology expertise for HP management, NTM assessment, and steroid tapering.

consults.name: Infectious Disease (if NTM treatment considered)
consults.result: Available if NTM treatment needed. Not indicated currently.
consults.reasoning: ID consult if true NTM pulmonary infection requiring multi-drug antibiotic therapy.

disposition.name: Outpatient management
disposition.result: Stable for outpatient treatment. Hot tub must be removed before discharge.
disposition.reasoning: Most HP managed outpatient. Admission only for severe hypoxia. Critical to ensure hot tub removal - otherwise patient returns to exposure.

disposition.name: Hot tub disposal instructions
disposition.result: Drain and remove hot tub completely. Clean sunroom/area. Cannot simply clean and reuse - biofilm is resistant.
disposition.reasoning: Crystal clear instructions: the hot tub cannot be saved. Must be removed. Enhanced cleaning does not work.

disposition.name: Follow-up plan
disposition.result: Pulmonology follow-up in 2-4 weeks. Repeat PFTs in 6-8 weeks. Expect improvement within 2-4 weeks of exposure removal.
disposition.reasoning: Follow for symptom and PFT improvement. Most recover well with exposure removal and steroids.

disposition.name: Wife evaluation
disposition.result: Wife should see physician for evaluation - she has symptoms too with lower exposure (dose-response relationship).
disposition.reasoning: Anyone exposed to the hot tub with respiratory symptoms needs evaluation.

disposition.name: Return precautions
disposition.result: Return for: worsening shortness of breath, fever despite steroids, oxygen requirement increasing.
disposition.reasoning: Should improve, not worsen. Worsening suggests ongoing exposure or complication.

disposition.name: Prognosis counseling
disposition.result: Counseled: With hot tub removal and treatment, most patients recover fully. Symptoms improve within weeks. If exposure continues, lung scarring becomes permanent.
disposition.reasoning: Good prognosis IF exposure removed. Emphasize that continued use leads to permanent damage.

disposition.name: Future hot tub use
disposition.result: May be able to use hot tubs in future IF: outdoor (good ventilation), meticulously maintained, infrequent use. But some people remain sensitized and cannot use any hot tub safely.
disposition.reasoning: Some patients tolerate outdoor hot tubs with good maintenance. Others remain sensitized. Discuss after recovery.

actualDiagnosis.name: Hot Tub Lung (NTM Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis)
keyFindings: Indoor hot tub installed 2 years ago (when symptoms started)
Daily hot tub use with poor ventilation
Symptoms RESOLVED on cruise (away from hot tub)
Symptoms RETURNED within a week of going home
teachingPoints: Hot tub lung is HP caused by NTM (usually MAC) in aerosolized hot tub water
Indoor hot tubs are worse - aerosols concentrate in enclosed space
Key clue: symptoms improve away from home, return when home
NTM form biofilms resistant to normal chlorine levels
Can be HP (hypersensitivity) or actual NTM lung infection
Treatment is REMOVAL of the hot tub (cleaning is not sufficient)
May need steroids for HP; antibiotics only if true infection
Similar pattern can occur with showers, humidifiers, swimming pools

closureScript.diagnosis: You have what's called 'hot tub lung' - your immune system is reacting to bacteria that grow in hot tub water. Every time you use the hot tub and breathe in the steam, you're exposing your lungs to these bacteria and triggering inflammation.
closureScript.plan: The most important treatment is getting rid of the hot tub. I know that's hard to hear since you love it, but your lungs will keep getting damaged as long as you're exposed. We'll also give you steroids to calm down the inflammation. You should start feeling better within days of removing the exposure.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: If you keep using the hot tub, you'll develop permanent scarring in your lungs. Even cleaning it won't be enough - these bacteria form films that are very hard to kill. Your wife should see a doctor too since she's having symptoms.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this isn't the answer you wanted. Some people try to just clean the hot tub better, but that rarely works - the bacteria are very resistant. If you really want a hot tub, it would need to be outdoors with good ventilation and extremely rigorous maintenance, but honestly the safest thing is to not have one.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Multiple infections this year. Recently moved to a new apartment with visible mold in the bathroom.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No recent illnesses other than this current presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Patient is dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has supportive spouse and family. Works full-time. Worried about missing work and health insurance.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Very health-conscious and proactive. Researches conditions online before appointments.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Occasional ER visits for minor issues. No recent hospitalizations.

male-28-histo.name: James Brown
male-28-histo.chiefComplaint: Terrible cough, fever, and chest pain for the past week
male-28-histo.variantDescription: Acute pulmonary histoplasmosis from bat cave exposure

missedConsequences.Histoplasmosis: Missed diagnosis leads to progressive pneumonia. In immunocompromised, can disseminate and become fatal.
missedConsequences.Bacterial Pneumonia: Undertreating bacterial infection leads to complications.

commonPitfalls: Treating as bacterial CAP without asking about exposures
Not asking about cave/bat/bird exposure
Missing the Ohio/Mississippi River Valley connection
Not recognizing that antibiotics won't help fungal infection
Not checking for immunocompromise

allDiagnoses.name: Histoplasmosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cave exploration + bat guano exposure + Ohio River Valley + pneumonia not responding to antibiotics = histoplasmosis.

allDiagnoses.name: Community-Acquired Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect response to antibiotics. Exposure history points to fungal cause.

allDiagnoses.name: Blastomycosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Possible in Ohio Valley but cave/bat exposure points to histo.

allDiagnoses.name: Tuberculosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Should be considered with pulmonary infiltrates and systemic symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cave exposure history makes fungal more likely.

male-28-histo.oldcartsNarrative: "I've had this terrible cough for about a week, with fevers up to 102, chest pain when I breathe, and I feel exhausted. Started about 10 days after I went caving with some friends in Kentucky. My doctor gave me azithromycin and it didn't help at all. The cough has gotten worse and now I'm getting short of breath. Several of my friends who were in the cave are sick too."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the cave trip
historyQuestions.answer: Went exploring caves in Kentucky about 2 weeks ago. We were in a cave with lots of bats. The floor was covered in bat droppings.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CAVE with BATS and BAT GUANO = prime histoplasma exposure. Classic presentation.

historyQuestions.text: Are your friends who went sick too?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! Three of the four of us are sick with similar symptoms.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Multiple people with same exposure = outbreak. Common with caving, chicken coops, construction.

historyQuestions.text: You said antibiotics didn't help?
historyQuestions.answer: No, the Z-pack did nothing. Still getting worse actually.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Failure to respond to antibacterials suggests fungal or viral etiology

historyQuestions.text: Describe your chest pain
historyQuestions.answer: Sharp pain when I take a deep breath. Worse on the right side.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pleuritic chest pain common in pulmonary histoplasmosis

historyQuestions.text: Any other symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: Terrible fatigue, headache, muscle aches. Feel like I have the flu but way worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Systemic symptoms common - can mimic flu

historyQuestions.text: Any problems with your immune system?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm healthy. Never been sick like this before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important - disseminated histo occurs in immunocompromised. He's immunocompetent.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just finished the azithromycin. Otherwise nothing regular. Some ibuprofen for the fever.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No immunosuppressive medications

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before starting antifungals if needed

historyQuestions.text: Any chronic medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Healthy as can be. Run marathons actually.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Immunocompetent host - likely to recover without antifungals

historyQuestions.text: Any risk factors for HIV or have you been tested recently?
historyQuestions.answer: I was tested last year for life insurance, it was negative. No risk factors.
historyQuestions.reasoning: HIV negative - important because disseminated histo is AIDS-defining

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

hobbies_hiking_camping.answer: I love outdoor adventures. This cave trip was a bucket list thing.
hobbies_hiking_camping.relevance: Recreational caving is a recognized risk factor for histoplasmosis.

geographic_recent_travel.answer: We drove to Kentucky for the cave trip. Beautiful area.
geographic_recent_travel.relevance: Kentucky is in the Ohio/Mississippi River Valley - ENDEMIC area for histoplasmosis. Highest rates in the country.

geographic_endemic_areas.answer: Specifically explored caves in central Kentucky with known bat populations.
geographic_endemic_areas.relevance: Bat caves are perfect for Histoplasma - the fungus grows in soil enriched with bat (or bird) guano.

occupation_current_ppe_use.answer: We wore headlamps and helmets but no masks. Didn't think we needed them.
occupation_current_ppe_use.relevance: NO respiratory protection while disturbing bat guano = massive inhalation of fungal spores.

social_household_others_sick.answer: Three of the four people who went caving are now sick with the same thing.
social_household_others_sick.relevance: Outbreak pattern - multiple people with shared exposure confirms environmental source.

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing, fatigued, mild respiratory distress
physicalExam.reasoning: Systemic illness

physicalExam.finding: BP 122/76, HR 92, RR 20, Temp 101.8°F, SpO2 94% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Fever and mild hypoxia indicate significant pulmonary involvement

physicalExam.finding: Decreased breath sounds at right base. Crackles bilateral lower lobes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Evidence of pulmonary infiltrates

physicalExam.finding: Shotty cervical lymphadenopathy
physicalExam.reasoning: Lymphadenopathy common in histoplasmosis

physicalExam.finding: No rashes or lesions
physicalExam.reasoning: No disseminated disease (would see skin lesions)

labs.name: Histoplasma urine antigen (and serum)
labs.result: Urine antigen positive. Serum antigen also positive.
labs.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC: Urine antigen is most sensitive test (>90%) for acute pulmonary histo. Cross-reacts with Blastomyces. Results in 1-2 days.

labs.name: Histoplasma serology (immunodiffusion, CF)
labs.reasoning: Serology takes 4-8 weeks to become positive. H and M bands on immunodiffusion. CF titer >1:32 suggests active disease.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 11.4 with mild lymphocytosis, Hgb 14.2, Plt 245
labs.reasoning: Mild leukocytosis common. Pancytopenia would suggest disseminated disease with bone marrow involvement.

labs.name: CMP including LDH
labs.reasoning: Elevated LDH can occur in histoplasmosis. Baseline before potential itraconazole therapy.

labs.name: HIV test
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL: Disseminated histoplasmosis is AIDS-defining illness. HIV changes treatment approach entirely.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Bilateral patchy infiltrates, most prominent in lower lobes. Hilar lymphadenopathy. No cavitation.
imaging.reasoning: Classic CXR pattern: infiltrates + hilar adenopathy in young patient from endemic area after exposure.

imaging.name: CT Chest
imaging.result: Multiple pulmonary nodules (5-15mm) with surrounding ground-glass. Bilateral hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. No cavitation.
imaging.reasoning: CT better defines extent of pulmonary disease. Pattern consistent with acute pulmonary histoplasmosis.

procedures.name: Bronchoscopy with BAL (if needed)
procedures.result: Not performed - diagnosis made by antigen testing.
procedures.reasoning: BAL can show yeast forms and can be cultured. Usually not needed when urine antigen positive.

treatments.name: Observation only (mild-moderate, immunocompetent)
treatments.result: This patient is immunocompetent with mild-moderate disease. Observation without antifungal treatment is appropriate. Most recover spontaneously.
treatments.reasoning: Mild-moderate acute pulmonary histoplasmosis in immunocompetent patients is SELF-LIMITED. Does not require antifungal therapy. Symptoms resolve in 2-4 weeks.

treatments.name: Itraconazole (if treatment indicated)
treatments.result: Would start itraconazole 200mg TID x 3 days, then 200mg BID x 6-12 weeks if: symptoms >4 weeks, moderate-severe, immunocompromised.
treatments.reasoning: Itraconazole for: prolonged symptoms (>1 month), moderate-severe disease (hypoxia, diffuse infiltrates), immunocompromised. Not needed for mild self-limited disease.

treatments.name: Amphotericin B (severe disease)
treatments.result: Not indicated for this patient. Reserve for severe pulmonary (ARDS, respiratory failure) or disseminated disease.
treatments.reasoning: Amphotericin B lipid formulation for: severe acute pulmonary with respiratory failure, disseminated disease, CNS involvement.

treatments.name: Corticosteroids (if severe inflammation)
treatments.result: Consider adding prednisone if significant respiratory distress or pericarditis.
treatments.reasoning: Steroids may help with severe inflammatory response (ARDS, pericarditis). Use with antifungal therapy.

treatments.name: Supportive care
treatments.result: Acetaminophen for fever, rest, hydration. Follow-up if not improving in 2 weeks.
treatments.reasoning: Most patients need only supportive care and time. Symptoms typically peak at 1-2 weeks and resolve by 4 weeks.

consults.name: Infectious Disease
consults.result: Confirms acute pulmonary histoplasmosis. Agrees with observation given mild disease and immunocompetent host. Will follow.
consults.reasoning: ID expertise for treatment decisions. Most mild cases don't need antifungals but judgment call.

disposition.name: Outpatient with close follow-up
disposition.result: Stable for outpatient management. Follow-up in 1-2 weeks. Return sooner if worsening.
disposition.reasoning: Mild-moderate acute pulmonary histoplasmosis in immunocompetent host managed outpatient. Most recover without treatment.

disposition.name: Admission criteria
disposition.result: Would admit for: hypoxia requiring oxygen, severe dyspnea, diffuse bilateral infiltrates, immunocompromised, hemodynamic instability.
disposition.reasoning: Severe acute pulmonary histoplasmosis can cause ARDS. Admit if significant respiratory compromise.

disposition.name: Return precautions
disposition.result: Return for: worsening dyspnea, high fevers persisting >2 weeks, new symptoms (headache, confusion, skin lesions suggesting dissemination).
disposition.reasoning: Should be improving by 2 weeks. Persistent or new symptoms warrant re-evaluation.

disposition.name: Follow-up plan
disposition.result: ID follow-up in 2 weeks. Repeat CXR in 4-6 weeks to document resolution. Antigen levels should decline.
disposition.reasoning: Follow for clinical improvement. CXR may take weeks to normalize. Calcified granulomas may persist as sequelae.

disposition.name: Outbreak reporting
disposition.result: Reported to public health as cluster/outbreak. Cave exposure documented. Other affected cavers should seek care.
disposition.reasoning: Multiple people with shared exposure = outbreak. Public health should be notified. Cave may need signage/warnings.

disposition.name: Prevention counseling
disposition.result: For future cave exploration or work in endemic areas: N95 respirator when disturbing soil, especially near bat/bird roosts.
disposition.reasoning: Prevention advice for future exposures. N95 provides protection against fungal spores.

disposition.name: Chronic sequelae to watch for
disposition.result: Counseled: Most recover completely. Some develop calcified granulomas or mediastinal fibrosis (rare). Follow up if new symptoms in future.
disposition.reasoning: Rare complications: mediastinal fibrosis (can cause SVC syndrome), pulmonary nodules mimicking cancer. Most have no long-term issues.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Pulmonary Histoplasmosis
keyFindings: Cave exploration in Kentucky (Ohio River Valley - endemic area)
teachingPoints: THINK HISTO with bat/bird exposure in Ohio/Mississippi River Valley
Classic exposures: caves, chicken coops, old buildings, construction
Urine antigen is most sensitive test for acute pulmonary disease
Antibiotics won't help - it's a fungus
Most immunocompetent patients recover without antifungals
Severe/prolonged symptoms or immunocompromise: treat with itraconazole or amphotericin
Report outbreaks to public health
Prevention: N95 mask when disturbing soil in endemic areas

closureScript.diagnosis: You have histoplasmosis - a fungal infection you got from breathing in spores in that bat cave. The bat droppings on the cave floor contain a fungus that, when disturbed, releases spores into the air. That's why multiple people from your trip are sick.
closureScript.plan: The good news is that in healthy people like you, this usually gets better on its own. We'll watch you closely - if you're not improving in a week or two, we'll start antifungal medication. The antibiotics you took couldn't work because this is a fungus, not bacteria.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back if you develop worsening shortness of breath, very high fevers, or any new symptoms like confusion or severe headache. Also let me know if you have any conditions affecting your immune system that you didn't mention.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: For future cave explorations, especially in caves with bats or in the Ohio/Mississippi River Valley area, wear an N95 mask. Your friends who are sick should see a doctor and mention the cave trip - they likely have the same thing.

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Guidelines for Histoplasmosis 2007
guidelineReferences.citation: Wheat LJ et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2007;45(7):807-825
keyPoints: Mild-moderate acute pulmonary: observation without treatment
Treat if symptoms >4 weeks or severe
Itraconazole for moderate disease requiring treatment
Amphotericin B for severe pulmonary or disseminated
Urine antigen most sensitive for acute disease

guidelineReferences.name: CDC Histoplasmosis Information
guidelineReferences.citation: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
keyPoints: Endemic in Ohio and Mississippi River Valleys
Associated with bat and bird droppings
N95 respirator for high-risk exposures
Report outbreaks to public health
Most infections are asymptomatic or mild

male-42-arizona.name: James Foster
male-42-arizona.chiefComplaint: Cough and fatigue for 6 weeks that won't go away
male-42-arizona.variantDescription: Recent move to Arizona with dust exposure, classic Valley Fever

missedConsequences.Coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever): Untreated Valley Fever can disseminate to meninges, bones, and skin. Meningitis is often fatal without treatment.
missedConsequences.Tuberculosis: Missed TB leads to continued transmission and progression to miliary disease.
missedConsequences.Lung Cancer: Delayed cancer diagnosis leads to advanced stage at presentation.

commonPitfalls: Not asking about recent relocation to endemic areas
Missing the connection between outdoor dusty work and fungal exposure
Not recognizing erythema nodosum as a clue to systemic fungal infection
Treating repeatedly with antibiotics without considering fungal etiology
Forgetting that newcomers to endemic areas are at highest risk

mcqs.question: The patient recently moved from Ohio to Arizona and has been doing outdoor landscaping. What is the most important geographic risk factor?

options.explanation: Altitude affects oxygenation but not fungal risk.
Coccidioides immitis lives in desert soil of the Southwest US. Disturbing soil (digging, construction, dust storms) releases spores.
Coccidioidomycosis is not waterborne.
Valley Fever is from soil fungus, not air pollution.

mcqs.question: The patient has painful red nodules on his shins and joint pain. What does this constellation suggest?

options.explanation: Cellulitis would be warm, spreading, and not typically bilateral on shins.

options.text: Erythema nodosum - suggests good immune response to coccidioidomycosis
options.explanation: Erythema nodosum + arthralgias ('desert rheumatism') is classic for Valley Fever and actually indicates a GOOD prognosis (strong immune response).
DVT is typically unilateral and not nodular.
RA nodules have different distribution and appearance.

allDiagnoses.name: Coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Recent move from non-endemic to endemic area + dusty outdoor work + 6-week pneumonia unresponsive to antibiotics + erythema nodosum + arthralgias = classic Valley Fever.

allDiagnoses.name: Tuberculosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Subacute pneumonia with cavitation requires TB rule-out.

allDiagnoses.name: Histoplasmosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Wrong geography - histoplasmosis is Ohio/Mississippi valley, not Arizona.

allDiagnoses.name: Lung Cancer
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cavitary lesion could represent cancer, though age and history favor infection.

allDiagnoses.name: Bacterial Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Failed two courses of antibiotics; erythema nodosum points to systemic fungal infection.

allDiagnoses.name: Sarcoidosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause similar findings but clear geographic exposure points to cocci.

male-42-arizona.oldcartsNarrative: "I've had this cough for about 6 weeks now. It started when I was getting settled into our new place. At first I thought it was just a cold, but it never went away. I've been to urgent care twice - they gave me amoxicillin first, then azithromycin. Neither helped. I've also had low-grade fevers off and on, and I'm exhausted. I've lost about 8 pounds. Then about 2 weeks ago these painful red bumps appeared on my shins, and my ankles and knees have been aching terribly."

historyQuestions.text: What is your cough like?
historyQuestions.answer: Dry at first, now I'm coughing up some yellowish stuff. No blood.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Productive cough with prolonged duration suggests ongoing infection

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the bumps on your legs
historyQuestions.answer: Painful red bumps on both shins. They appeared about 2 weeks ago. Very tender.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Erythema nodosum - classic associated finding with Valley Fever

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your joint pain
historyQuestions.answer: My ankles and knees ache. Started around when the bumps appeared. Feels like flu aches but won't stop.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 'Desert rheumatism' - arthralgias associated with Valley Fever

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned weight loss?
historyQuestions.answer: About 8 pounds without trying. No appetite.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Constitutional symptoms support systemic infection

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your fevers
historyQuestions.answer: Low-grade, usually in the afternoon. 100-101. Night sweats too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Afternoon fevers and night sweats suggest granulomatous infection

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: Never smoked.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Non-smoker makes cancer less likely

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just finished azithromycin that didn't help. Otherwise nothing - I've been healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No immunosuppressive medications

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before starting antifungal therapy

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Healthy. Had a physical before the move and everything was perfect.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Immunocompetent - important for prognosis

historyQuestions.text: Any problems with your immune system? Taking steroids or other medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that. Always been healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Immunosuppression dramatically increases disseminated cocci risk

historyQuestions.text: What is your ethnic background?
historyQuestions.answer: Filipino on my mom's side, mixed European on my dad's.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Filipino and African ancestry have higher risk for disseminated disease

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

geographic_places_lived.answer: Grew up in Ohio, lived there my whole life until 2 months ago. Just moved to Arizona.
geographic_places_lived.relevance: Recent migration from non-endemic to endemic area is HIGH RISK. People who grew up in endemic areas often have partial immunity; newcomers have none.

geographic_endemic_areas.answer: We're about 30 miles outside Phoenix. Desert area.
geographic_endemic_areas.relevance: Sonoran Desert of Arizona is one of the highest-risk areas for coccidioidomycosis in the world.

occupation_current_work_outdoors.answer: I've been doing all the landscaping myself. Digging post holes, grading the driveway, moving dirt.
occupation_current_work_outdoors.relevance: Soil disruption releases Coccidioides spores. Digging, construction, and dust exposure are major risk factors.

air_local_air_quality_notice.answer: It's incredibly dusty here. Dust storms, dust everywhere. Much worse than Ohio.
air_local_air_quality_notice.relevance: Dust storms (haboobs) in Arizona spread Coccidioides spores over wide areas.

occupation_current_ppe_use.answer: No mask. I didn't think I needed one for yard work.
occupation_current_ppe_use.relevance: No respiratory protection during dusty work = maximal spore inhalation.

physicalExam.finding: Fatigued-appearing man, no acute distress
physicalExam.reasoning: Chronic illness appearance

physicalExam.finding: BP 122/78, HR 82, RR 18, Temp 100.4°F, SpO2 96% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Low-grade fever, mild hypoxia

physicalExam.finding: Multiple tender, erythematous nodules on bilateral shins - classic erythema nodosum
physicalExam.reasoning: Erythema nodosum is a classic manifestation of Valley Fever

physicalExam.finding: Scattered crackles in right upper lobe
physicalExam.reasoning: Upper lobe involvement is common in Valley Fever

physicalExam.finding: Mild swelling and tenderness of bilateral ankles, no effusion
physicalExam.reasoning: Desert rheumatism - reactive arthralgias

physicalExam.finding: Shotty cervical nodes bilaterally
physicalExam.reasoning: Reactive lymphadenopathy

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.reasoning: Eosinophilia is common in coccidioidomycosis and helpful clue. Also in other fungi and parasites.

labs.name: Coccidioides serology (IgM, IgG, CF titer)
labs.result: IgM positive, IgG positive, complement fixation titer 1:16
labs.reasoning: Serology is primary diagnostic test. CF titer >1:16 associated with higher risk of dissemination. Titer tracks disease activity.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Baseline assessment before azole therapy. Azoles can cause electrolyte abnormalities.

labs.name: LFTs
labs.reasoning: Baseline before azole therapy - fluconazole is hepatically metabolized and can cause hepatotoxicity.

labs.name: ESR and CRP
labs.reasoning: Inflammatory markers elevated in active infection. Can use to monitor response.

labs.name: HIV test
labs.reasoning: HIV dramatically increases risk of disseminated coccidioidomycosis. Screen all patients.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Right upper lobe infiltrate with a thin-walled cavity. Right hilar prominence.
imaging.reasoning: Cavitary lesion is common in pulmonary coccidioidomycosis. Upper lobe predilection.

imaging.name: CT Chest
imaging.result: Right upper lobe consolidation with 2.5cm thin-walled cavity. Hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Small nodules in left upper lobe.
imaging.reasoning: CT defines extent of disease. Thin-walled cavities characteristic of cocci (vs thick-walled in TB). Adenopathy common.

procedures.name: Sputum fungal culture
procedures.result: Pending (takes 1-3 weeks) - Coccidioides immitis eventually grows
procedures.reasoning: Culture confirms diagnosis but takes weeks. Handle with care - lab should be notified (BSL-3 organism).

procedures.name: Bronchoscopy with BAL (if needed)
procedures.result: Not performed - diagnosis made by serology.
procedures.reasoning: Consider if serology negative but high clinical suspicion. Can see spherules on BAL cytology.

procedures.name: Lumbar puncture (if dissemination suspected)
procedures.result: Not indicated - no meningeal symptoms.
procedures.reasoning: LP indicated if headache, neck stiffness, confusion - cocci meningitis is life-threatening. CSF would show lymphocytic pleocytosis, low glucose, positive CF.

treatments.name: Fluconazole 400mg PO daily (TREATMENT)
treatments.result: Fluconazole 400mg daily started. Continue for minimum 3-6 months. Longer if CF titer remains elevated.
treatments.reasoning: Fluconazole is first-line for moderate pulmonary cocci with cavitation. Duration guided by symptoms and CF titer. May need 12+ months.

treatments.name: Itraconazole (alternative azole)
treatments.result: Alternative if fluconazole not tolerated. Requires therapeutic drug monitoring.
treatments.reasoning: Second-line oral azole. Better for bone/joint cocci. Requires levels due to variable absorption.

treatments.name: Amphotericin B (for severe/meningeal)
treatments.result: Not indicated for this presentation.
treatments.reasoning: Reserve amphotericin for: severe pneumonia, meningitis (intrathecal), rapidly progressive dissemination, azole failure.

treatments.name: NSAIDs for erythema nodosum/arthralgias
treatments.result: Ibuprofen 600mg TID for shin pain and joint aches. Very effective for desert rheumatism.
treatments.reasoning: Erythema nodosum and arthralgias are self-limited but painful. NSAIDs provide excellent symptomatic relief.

treatments.name: Observation only (mild cases)
treatments.result: This patient requires treatment given cavity and elevated CF titer. Observation appropriate for mild self-limited cases.
treatments.reasoning: 60% of cocci infections are asymptomatic/self-limited. Treat if: symptoms >6 weeks, severe, cavitary, CF titer >1:16, risk factors for dissemination.

consults.name: Infectious Disease consult
consults.result: Confirms Valley Fever diagnosis. Recommends fluconazole given cavity and elevated CF titer. Will follow CF titers q3 months.
consults.reasoning: ID expertise valuable for treatment duration, monitoring, and recognizing dissemination.

disposition.name: Outpatient management
disposition.result: Stable for outpatient treatment. Close follow-up arranged.
disposition.reasoning: Most pulmonary cocci managed outpatient. Admit for: severe hypoxia, meningitis, rapidly progressive disease, immunocompromised.

disposition.name: Follow-up plan
disposition.result: ID follow-up in 4 weeks. Repeat CF titer q3 months. Repeat CXR in 2-3 months to ensure cavity resolution.
disposition.reasoning: CF titer is key monitoring tool - should decrease with successful treatment. Rising titer indicates treatment failure or dissemination.

disposition.name: Dissemination warning signs
disposition.result: Return for: severe headache, neck stiffness, new skin lesions, bone/joint swelling, worsening symptoms.
disposition.reasoning: Disseminated cocci can involve meninges (life-threatening), bones, joints, skin. Patient must know warning signs. Filipino ancestry increases his risk.

disposition.name: Prevention counseling
disposition.result: When doing dusty outdoor work in Arizona, wear N95 mask. Avoid outdoor activity during dust storms.
disposition.reasoning: No vaccine available. Respiratory protection during high-exposure activities reduces future risk.

disposition.name: Treatment duration counseling
disposition.result: Counseled that antifungal treatment will be minimum 3-6 months, possibly longer. Do not stop early even if feeling better.
disposition.reasoning: Early treatment discontinuation leads to relapse. Duration guided by symptom resolution AND CF titer decrease.

disposition.name: LFT monitoring
disposition.result: Will check LFTs monthly while on fluconazole. Stop and call if jaundice or dark urine.
disposition.reasoning: Azoles are hepatotoxic. Monitor LFTs regularly during treatment.

actualDiagnosis.name: Pulmonary Coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever)
keyFindings: Recent migration from Ohio to Arizona (non-endemic to endemic)
Heavy dust exposure from outdoor digging/landscaping
teachingPoints: Valley Fever is endemic to Southwest US (Arizona, California Central Valley)
Newcomers to endemic areas are at HIGHEST risk (no prior immunity)
Classic triad: pneumonia + erythema nodosum + arthralgias
Erythema nodosum actually indicates GOOD prognosis (strong immune response)
Risk factors for dissemination: Filipino/African ancestry, immunosuppression, pregnancy, diabetes
Cavities are common and usually resolve without treatment
Serology (IgM, IgG, complement fixation) is diagnostic
Most cases self-resolve; fluconazole for severe/prolonged/high-risk

closureScript.diagnosis: You have Valley Fever - a fungal infection you got from breathing in spores from the desert soil while doing your landscaping. The good news is that the rash on your legs actually means your immune system is fighting it well.
closureScript.plan: Because you have a cavity in your lung and your antibody levels are elevated, I'm going to start you on an antifungal medication called fluconazole. You'll need to take this for at least 3-6 months, and we'll monitor your antibody levels to make sure the infection is resolving.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Most people do very well with Valley Fever, but we need to watch for spread to other parts of the body. Come back immediately if you develop severe headaches, neck stiffness, or skin lesions other than the shin bumps.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: This isn't contagious person-to-person, so your family isn't at risk from you. When you're doing dusty outdoor work in Arizona in the future, wearing an N95 mask can help reduce your risk of reinfection.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Moved to Arizona 2 months ago from the Midwest. Has been doing a lot of outdoor hiking and yard work.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: Felt like he had "the flu" about a week before this started - body aches, fatigue, low fever that resolved.
patientBackground.travelHistory: Recently moved from Ohio to Arizona 2 months ago. No international travel.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Patient is dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has supportive spouse and family. Works full-time. Worried about missing work and health insurance.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Tends to wait and see if things get better on their own before seeking care. Doesn't like taking medications.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Occasional ER visits for minor issues. No recent hospitalizations.

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

male-55-outdoorsman.name: Robert King
male-55-outdoorsman.chiefComplaint: Cough, fever, and these strange sores on my skin
male-55-outdoorsman.variantDescription: Hunter/fisherman in Wisconsin with pulmonary and cutaneous blastomycosis

missedConsequences.Blastomycosis: Untreated blastomycosis can disseminate to CNS, bones, and genitourinary tract. Mortality without treatment is significant.
missedConsequences.Tuberculosis: Missed TB leads to continued transmission and progression.
missedConsequences.Lung Cancer: Delayed cancer diagnosis in cavitary lung lesion.

commonPitfalls: Not asking about outdoor activities in endemic areas
Treating repeatedly with antibiotics without considering fungal etiology
Missing the connection between skin lesions and pulmonary disease
Not recognizing that blastomycosis mimics lung cancer on imaging
Forgetting that blasto skin lesions are verrucous (warty), not ulcerative

mcqs.question: The patient is an avid hunter and fisherman in Wisconsin near the Mississippi River. What fungal infection should you consider?

options.explanation: Aspergillosis is not endemic to this region.
Blastomyces dermatitidis is endemic to the Mississippi/Ohio River valleys and Great Lakes region. It lives in moist soil near waterways.
Cocci is in the Southwest, not Wisconsin.
Cryptococcus is associated with pigeon droppings, not this geographic area.

mcqs.question: The skin lesions are verrucous (warty) with raised borders and central clearing. What do they represent?

options.explanation: SCC is possible but disseminated lesions with pulmonary disease suggest infection.
Verrucous skin lesions with raised, crusted borders are characteristic of cutaneous blastomycosis - the most common extrapulmonary manifestation.
Psoriasis wouldn't explain the pulmonary findings.
Cutaneous TB looks different and is rare.

allDiagnoses.name: Blastomycosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Northern Wisconsin near rivers + hunting/fishing + mass-like pneumonia + verrucous skin lesions = classic blastomycosis.

allDiagnoses.name: Tuberculosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cavitary/mass-like pneumonia requires TB rule-out.

allDiagnoses.name: Lung Cancer
allDiagnoses.rationale: Mass-like consolidation could be cancer; non-smoker makes it less likely.

allDiagnoses.name: Histoplasmosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Similar geography but skin involvement is less common with histo.

allDiagnoses.name: Nocardiosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: More common in immunocompromised patients.

male-55-outdoorsman.oldcartsNarrative: "I've had this cough for about 3 weeks now. Started dry but now I'm bringing up yellowish stuff, sometimes blood-tinged. I've had fevers, night sweats, lost about 10 pounds. I went to urgent care twice - got azithromycin, then amoxicillin. Neither helped. Then about a week ago these sores appeared on my face and arm - they look like warts almost, raised and crusty. I'm outdoors all the time - hunting, fishing. I live up in northern Wisconsin."

historyQuestions.text: Describe your cough
historyQuestions.answer: Productive cough for 3 weeks. Sometimes blood-tinged. Can't shake it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prolonged productive cough with hemoptysis

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the skin sores
historyQuestions.answer: Started as a small bump on my face. Now it's crusted over and spreading. Same thing on my arm.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Verrucous skin lesions are classic for cutaneous blastomycosis

historyQuestions.text: What antibiotics have you tried?
historyQuestions.answer: Azithromycin first, then amoxicillin. Neither helped.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Failure of antibacterials suggests fungal or mycobacterial cause

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your outdoor activities
historyQuestions.answer: I hunt and fish constantly. I'm always in the woods, along the rivers.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Outdoor activities near waterways = high-risk exposure

historyQuestions.text: Where exactly in Wisconsin?
historyQuestions.answer: Northern Wisconsin, near the Mississippi River. Lots of forests and wetlands.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Endemic area for Blastomyces

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: Never smoked a cigarette in my life.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Non-smoker makes cancer less likely

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

geographic_places_lived.answer: Born and raised in northern Wisconsin, near the Mississippi River. Never left.
geographic_places_lived.relevance: Wisconsin and the Mississippi/Ohio River valleys are endemic for blastomycosis. The fungus lives in moist soil near waterways.

hobbies_hunting_fishing.answer: I hunt deer every fall. Fish all summer. Always in the woods.
hobbies_hunting_fishing.relevance: Outdoor activities in endemic areas, especially near water, increase exposure to Blastomyces in soil.

hobbies_hiking_camping.answer: I camp and hike along the rivers. Beautiful country up here.
hobbies_hiking_camping.relevance: Riverbank soil is particularly rich in Blastomyces.

occupation_current_job_title.answer: I work for the forest service. Outdoors every day.
occupation_current_job_title.relevance: Occupational outdoor exposure in endemic area.

physicalExam.finding: Chronically ill-appearing man, weight loss evident
physicalExam.reasoning: Signs of chronic illness

physicalExam.finding: BP 132/82, HR 94, RR 22, Temp 101.4°F, SpO2 92% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Fever, tachypnea, hypoxia

physicalExam.finding: Two verrucous (warty), crusted plaques with violaceous borders - one on right cheek (2cm), one on left forearm (3cm). Central clearing with raised edges.
physicalExam.reasoning: Verrucous skin lesions are characteristic of cutaneous blastomycosis

physicalExam.finding: Crackles and bronchial breath sounds in right upper lobe
physicalExam.reasoning: Consolidation in upper lobe

physicalExam.finding: Shotty cervical lymphadenopathy
physicalExam.reasoning: Reactive nodes

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis supports infection. Mild anemia of chronic disease may be present.

labs.name: Blastomyces urine antigen
labs.reasoning: Rapid diagnostic test - highly sensitive (90%+). Cross-reacts with Histoplasma (both are endemic fungi). Results in days vs weeks for culture.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Baseline renal function before antifungal therapy. Itraconazole is renally cleared.

labs.name: LFTs
labs.reasoning: Baseline before azole therapy. Itraconazole is hepatotoxic - need baseline and monitoring.

labs.name: HIV test
labs.reasoning: HIV increases risk of disseminated blastomycosis. Important for prognosis and treatment decisions.

labs.name: Sputum AFB smear and culture
labs.reasoning: Must rule out TB given similar presentation and overlapping geography.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Right upper lobe mass-like consolidation with air bronchograms. No cavitation.
imaging.reasoning: Mass-like consolidation is common appearance of blastomycosis - often initially mistaken for lung cancer.

imaging.name: CT Chest with contrast
imaging.result: Right upper lobe mass-like consolidation (5cm) with surrounding ground-glass opacity. Hilar adenopathy. Additional smaller nodules in both lungs.
imaging.reasoning: Defines extent of pulmonary disease. Multi-focal disease is common. Pattern mimics malignancy - biopsy needed.

imaging.name: MRI Brain (if CNS symptoms)
imaging.result: Not indicated - no neurological symptoms.
imaging.reasoning: CNS blastomycosis is rare but serious. MRI if headache, focal neuro deficits, or altered mental status.

procedures.name: Skin biopsy
procedures.result: Pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia with granulomatous inflammation. GMS stain shows broad-based budding yeast (8-15μm) consistent with Blastomyces dermatitidis.
procedures.reasoning: Tissue diagnosis is gold standard. BROAD-BASED budding is key morphology (vs narrow-based in Cryptococcus). Skin is easy to biopsy when lesions present.

procedures.name: Sputum fungal culture
procedures.result: Pending - Blastomyces grows in 2-4 weeks
procedures.reasoning: Culture confirms species but takes weeks. Treatment shouldn't wait for culture if clinical picture is classic.

procedures.name: Bronchoscopy with BAL (if sputum non-diagnostic)
procedures.result: Not performed - skin biopsy diagnostic.
procedures.reasoning: Consider if no accessible skin lesions and sputum non-diagnostic. BAL can show yeast and be cultured.

treatments.name: Itraconazole 200mg PO TID x 3 days, then 200mg BID (FIRST-LINE)
treatments.result: Itraconazole solution 200mg TID x 3 days loading, then 200mg BID for 6-12 months.
treatments.reasoning: Itraconazole is first-line for mild-moderate pulmonary and extrapulmonary blastomycosis. Loading dose important. Continue 6-12 months. Check trough levels (goal 1-2 μg/mL).

treatments.name: Amphotericin B lipid formulation (for severe disease)
treatments.result: Not indicated for this patient. Reserve for severe pulmonary or CNS disease.
treatments.reasoning: Amphotericin B for: severe pulmonary (ARDS, diffuse infiltrates), CNS involvement, immunocompromised. Step down to itraconazole after clinical improvement.

treatments.name: Voriconazole (alternative/CNS penetration)
treatments.result: Alternative azole. Better CNS penetration than itraconazole.
treatments.reasoning: Second-line azole option. Better CNS penetration if CNS disease suspected.

treatments.name: Note: Fluconazole less effective
treatments.result: Do not use fluconazole for blastomycosis - less active against Blastomyces than itraconazole.
treatments.reasoning: Unlike other endemic mycoses, blastomycosis responds poorly to fluconazole. Itraconazole strongly preferred.

treatments.name: Supportive care
treatments.result: Supplemental oxygen as needed. Nutritional support given weight loss.
treatments.reasoning: Address hypoxia and malnutrition from chronic infection.

consults.name: Infectious Disease
consults.result: Confirms pulmonary and cutaneous blastomycosis. Agrees with itraconazole. Will monitor trough levels and treatment response.
consults.reasoning: ID expertise for prolonged antifungal therapy, drug level monitoring, and treatment duration.

consults.name: Pulmonology (if bronchoscopy needed)
consults.result: Not needed - diagnosis made from skin biopsy.
consults.reasoning: Pulm consult if bronchoscopy needed for tissue diagnosis.

disposition.name: Outpatient management
disposition.result: Stable for outpatient treatment. Mild-moderate disease, not hypoxic at rest. Close follow-up arranged.
disposition.reasoning: Mild-moderate blastomycosis can be treated outpatient. Admit if: severe hypoxia, ARDS, CNS involvement, unable to take oral meds.

disposition.name: Treatment monitoring
disposition.result: Check itraconazole trough level at 2 weeks (goal 1-2 μg/mL). LFTs monthly. Clinical reassessment in 2-4 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Itraconazole has variable absorption - levels ensure therapeutic dosing. Monitor for hepatotoxicity.

disposition.name: Treatment duration counseling
disposition.result: Treatment for 6-12 months minimum. Do not stop early even if feeling better - relapse is common with short courses.
disposition.reasoning: Prolonged treatment needed. Skin lesions resolve in weeks but pulmonary disease requires months. Relapse with inadequate therapy.

disposition.name: Drug interaction counseling
disposition.result: Counseled about itraconazole drug interactions: avoid PPIs, H2 blockers (reduce absorption). Take with food/cola. Avoid certain statins, some antiarrhythmics.
disposition.reasoning: Itraconazole has many drug interactions. Requires acid for absorption - avoid antacids. CYP3A4 inhibitor.

disposition.name: Return precautions
disposition.result: Return for: worsening dyspnea, new headache or confusion (CNS spread), new skin lesions, medication side effects (nausea, jaundice).
disposition.reasoning: CNS spread is serious complication. Hepatotoxicity from itraconazole needs monitoring.

disposition.name: Follow-up imaging
disposition.result: Repeat CT chest in 2-3 months to document improvement. Expect slow radiographic resolution.
disposition.reasoning: Radiographic improvement lags behind clinical improvement. Mass-like opacity may take months to resolve.

actualDiagnosis.name: Pulmonary and Cutaneous Blastomycosis
keyFindings: Lives in northern Wisconsin near Mississippi River (endemic)
Verrucous, crusted skin lesions with raised borders
teachingPoints: Blastomycosis is endemic to Mississippi/Ohio River valleys and Great Lakes
Lives in moist soil, especially near waterways
Pulmonary infection primary; skin is most common extrapulmonary site
Skin lesions are VERRUCOUS (warty) with raised borders
Often mimics lung cancer on imaging
Diagnosis: see broad-based budding yeast on microscopy (vs narrow-based in Crypto)
Urine antigen test available (cross-reacts with histo)
Itraconazole for mild-moderate; amphotericin for severe/CNS

closureScript.diagnosis: You have a fungal infection called blastomycosis. This is a fungus that lives in the soil near rivers and waterways - exactly where you spend your time hunting and fishing. You likely breathed in the spores, and it's infected your lungs and spread to your skin.
closureScript.plan: I'm going to start you on an antifungal medication called itraconazole. You'll need to take it for 6-12 months. The skin lesions should clear up, and your lungs should heal.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: This infection can spread to other parts of the body, so we need to watch for any new symptoms - especially severe headaches that could suggest spread to the brain. Come back if you develop new skin lesions or worsening symptoms.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: This isn't contagious person-to-person. You got it from the environment. You can still enjoy your outdoor activities, but it's good to be aware that the fungus is in the soil in your area.

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Guidelines for Blastomycosis 2008
guidelineReferences.citation: Chapman SW et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2008;46(12):1801-1812
keyPoints: Itraconazole first-line for mild-moderate disease
Loading dose: 200mg TID x 3 days, then 200mg BID
Treatment duration: 6-12 months
Amphotericin B for severe or CNS disease
Monitor itraconazole trough levels (goal 1-2 μg/mL)

guidelineReferences.name: CDC Blastomycosis Information
guidelineReferences.citation: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
keyPoints: Endemic to Mississippi/Ohio River valleys and Great Lakes
Found in moist soil near waterways
Not contagious person-to-person
Broad-based budding yeast on microscopy
Can disseminate to skin, bone, CNS

male-35-baker.name: James Martinez
male-35-baker.chiefComplaint: Wheezing and chest tightness at work that gets better on weekends
male-35-baker.variantDescription: Commercial baker with flour-induced occupational asthma

missedConsequences.Occupational Asthma: Continued exposure leads to permanent asthma even after removal from workplace. Early diagnosis and removal essential for reversibility.
missedConsequences.Adult-Onset Asthma: Uncontrolled asthma causes airway remodeling and decline in lung function.

commonPitfalls: Not asking about symptom pattern relative to work schedule
Not recognizing the improvement on weekends/vacations as diagnostic clue
Missing the connection between flour dust and respiratory sensitization
Treating with inhalers without addressing the cause
Not involving occupational medicine for documentation and removal from exposure

mcqs.question: The patient's symptoms are worst during the workweek and improve on weekends. What should this pattern make you consider?

options.explanation: Stress can worsen asthma but wouldn't explain this clear work pattern.
Symptoms that improve AWAY from work (weekends, vacations) is the hallmark of occupational asthma. This pattern distinguishes it from work-aggravated asthma.
Would be triggered by exertion regardless of location.
Nocturnal asthma doesn't follow work schedule.

mcqs.question: The patient works in a commercial bakery with high flour dust exposure. What is the most likely sensitizing agent?

options.explanation: Yeast can cause sensitization but is less common than flour.
Baker's asthma is primarily caused by IgE-mediated sensitization to wheat flour proteins and flour additives like alpha-amylase enzyme.
Sugar is not a respiratory sensitizer.
Could cause irritant-induced symptoms but wouldn't explain this pattern.

allDiagnoses.name: Occupational Asthma (Baker's Asthma)
allDiagnoses.rationale: New baker + flour dust exposure + symptoms worse at work, better weekends + no prior asthma history = classic occupational asthma.

allDiagnoses.name: New-Onset Adult Asthma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Work-related pattern strongly suggests occupational cause.

allDiagnoses.name: GERD-related Cough/Wheeze
allDiagnoses.rationale: Work-related pattern doesn't fit GERD.

allDiagnoses.name: Vocal Cord Dysfunction
allDiagnoses.rationale: True wheezes on exam suggest asthma, not VCD.

male-35-baker.oldcartsNarrative: "I've had this wheezing and tight feeling in my chest for about 6 months now. It's definitely worse when I'm at work - by mid-week I'm really struggling. But here's the thing - by Sunday I feel almost normal. Then Monday it starts all over again. I took a week off for vacation last month and I felt great the whole time. I never had asthma before - I started this job about 8 months ago. I don't smoke. A few other guys at work cough a lot too."

historyQuestions.text: Describe your symptoms
historyQuestions.answer: Wheezing, tight chest, cough. Sometimes my eyes water too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic respiratory symptoms plus ocular symptoms suggest allergy

historyQuestions.text: When are symptoms worst?
historyQuestions.answer: Worst during work, especially after a few hours. Better when I leave.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Work-related pattern is key diagnostic feature

historyQuestions.text: You said weekends are better?
historyQuestions.answer: By Sunday I feel almost normal. Then Monday it starts all over again.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Improvement away from work is hallmark of occupational asthma

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your job
historyQuestions.answer: I'm a baker now. I started at a commercial bakery about 8 months ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: New job temporally related to symptom onset

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had asthma or allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: Never had any breathing problems before this job. No allergies I know of.
historyQuestions.reasoning: New onset with new job supports occupational etiology

historyQuestions.text: Do any coworkers have similar symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: A few other bakers complain about the same thing. The veteran guys seem fine though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Coworker symptoms support workplace cause; 'survivor bias' in veterans

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupation_current_job_title.answer: Baker at a commercial bakery. Making bread, pastries.
occupation_current_job_title.relevance: Baker's asthma is one of the most common forms of occupational asthma. Caused by wheat/flour allergens.

occupation_current_workplace_exposures.answer: Lots of flour dust. It gets everywhere. Sometimes you can see it floating in the air.
occupation_current_workplace_exposures.relevance: Visible flour dust indicates very high exposure levels. Wheat flour, rye flour, and flour additives (amylase) are potent respiratory sensitizers.

occupation_current_ppe_use.answer: We're supposed to wear masks but nobody does. It's hot and uncomfortable.
occupation_current_ppe_use.relevance: Lack of respiratory protection in high-exposure environment leads to sensitization.

occupation_current_workplace_ventilation.answer: There's some ventilation but when we're mixing big batches, the dust is everywhere.
occupation_current_workplace_ventilation.relevance: Inadequate ventilation during high-exposure tasks (mixing) concentrates allergens.

occupation_current_coworkers_sick.answer: Some of the newer guys cough a lot. The old-timers say you 'get used to it' but I don't think that's true.
occupation_current_coworkers_sick.relevance: 'Getting used to it' is a myth - veteran workers likely have survivor bias (sensitized workers quit or transfer).

physicalExam.finding: Well-appearing man, no acute distress (examined on weekend)
physicalExam.reasoning: Symptoms may be less evident when away from work

physicalExam.finding: BP 124/78, HR 82, RR 18, Temp 98.6°F, SpO2 97% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: May be near-normal between exposures

physicalExam.finding: Mild scattered expiratory wheezes bilaterally. No crackles.
physicalExam.reasoning: Wheezes even on weekend suggest ongoing airway inflammation

physicalExam.finding: Pale, boggy nasal turbinates
physicalExam.reasoning: Allergic rhinitis often accompanies occupational asthma

physicalExam.finding: Mild conjunctival injection
physicalExam.reasoning: Allergic conjunctivitis

labs.name: Specific IgE to wheat, rye, alpha-amylase
labs.result: Positive IgE to wheat flour and alpha-amylase
labs.reasoning: Confirms sensitization to occupational allergens

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.reasoning: Eosinophilia supports allergic etiology

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Normal chest radiograph. No infiltrates or hyperinflation.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out other pathology

procedures.name: Spirometry with bronchodilator
procedures.result: FEV1 78% predicted, FEV1/FVC 68%. Post-bronchodilator FEV1 improves 15%.
procedures.reasoning: Confirms obstructive pattern with reversibility

procedures.name: Serial peak flow monitoring
procedures.result: PEF drops 25% during workweek, recovers on weekends. Pattern highly suggestive of occupational asthma.
procedures.reasoning: Gold standard - documents work-relatedness

procedures.name: Methacholine challenge
procedures.result: Positive - PC20 2.4mg/mL
procedures.reasoning: Confirms bronchial hyperresponsiveness

consults.name: Occupational Medicine
consults.result: Confirms occupational asthma, recommends removal from exposure and workers' comp documentation.
consults.reasoning: Essential for proper documentation and work accommodations

consults.name: Pulmonology
consults.result: Agrees with diagnosis, recommends inhaled steroids and removal from exposure.
consults.reasoning: Long-term asthma management

treatments.name: Inhaled Corticosteroid (budesonide/fluticasone)
treatments.result: Started on fluticasone 250mcg BID. ICS is maintenance therapy for persistent asthma.
treatments.reasoning: Inhaled corticosteroids reduce airway inflammation and prevent exacerbations. First-line controller medication for persistent asthma.

treatments.name: Short-acting beta-agonist (albuterol)
treatments.result: Albuterol inhaler PRN for rescue. Use before work if returning to exposure (not recommended).
treatments.reasoning: Rescue inhaler for acute symptoms. Pre-treatment before exposure offers some protection but doesn't prevent sensitization.

treatments.name: Long-acting beta-agonist (if needed)
treatments.result: May add formoterol or salmeterol if ICS alone insufficient. Use as combination inhaler.
treatments.reasoning: LABA added if ICS alone doesn't control symptoms. Always use with ICS, never as monotherapy.

treatments.name: Nasal corticosteroid spray
treatments.result: Fluticasone nasal spray for allergic rhinitis symptoms.
treatments.reasoning: Occupational rhinitis often accompanies occupational asthma. Treating rhinitis may improve asthma control.

treatments.name: Antihistamine (as needed)
treatments.result: Cetirizine or loratadine for allergic symptoms (rhinitis, conjunctivitis).
treatments.reasoning: Non-sedating antihistamines help with allergic symptoms but don't treat asthma.

treatments.name: REMOVAL FROM FLOUR EXPOSURE (CRITICAL)
treatments.result: Strongly recommended to transfer to non-flour role or change jobs. THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT INTERVENTION.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL: Early removal from exposure can REVERSE occupational asthma. Continued exposure leads to PERMANENT disease. Medications alone are not enough.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with close follow-up
disposition.result: Follow-up in 2-4 weeks to assess response to therapy. Repeat spirometry in 3-6 months.
disposition.reasoning: Most occupational asthma managed outpatient. Serial PFTs monitor disease course.

disposition.name: Work Restrictions
disposition.result: Written medical restriction from flour exposure. Documentation for employer and workers' comp.
disposition.reasoning: Formal work restrictions protect patient legally and facilitate job accommodations or reassignment.

disposition.name: Workers' Compensation Filing
disposition.result: Referral to help file workers' comp claim. Work-related illness should be covered.
disposition.reasoning: Occupational asthma is a compensable work-related illness. Covers medical costs and may provide disability benefits if job change required.

disposition.name: Asthma Action Plan
disposition.result: Written plan with green/yellow/red zones, when to use rescue inhaler, when to seek care.
disposition.reasoning: All asthma patients should have written action plan. Improves outcomes and self-management.

disposition.name: Return Precautions
disposition.result: Return if symptoms worsen despite treatment, increasing rescue inhaler use, chest tightness not responding to albuterol.
disposition.reasoning: Worsening symptoms may indicate disease progression or poor exposure control.

disposition.name: Prognosis Counseling
disposition.result: Counseled that early removal from exposure offers best prognosis. If removed early, ~1/3 fully recover, ~1/3 improve significantly, ~1/3 have persistent symptoms.
disposition.reasoning: Patient needs to understand that continued exposure guarantees permanent asthma, while early removal offers chance of recovery.

actualDiagnosis.name: Occupational Asthma (Baker's Asthma)
keyFindings: New job as commercial baker 8 months ago
High flour dust exposure without respiratory protection
Symptoms worse during workweek, improve on weekends/vacations
Positive specific IgE to wheat flour and alpha-amylase
teachingPoints: Baker's asthma is a leading cause of occupational asthma
Caused by IgE-mediated sensitization to wheat, rye, or flour additives (amylase)
Key clue: symptoms IMPROVE away from work (weekends, vacations)
10-20% of bakers develop sensitization
Diagnosis: serial PFTs or PEF monitoring at work vs away from work
EARLY removal from exposure can REVERSE disease
Continued exposure leads to PERMANENT asthma
Workers' compensation should be pursued

closureScript.diagnosis: You have what's called 'baker's asthma' - an allergic reaction to the flour and enzymes you're exposed to at work. Your immune system has become sensitized to these proteins, and now when you breathe them in, your airways constrict.
closureScript.plan: The most important thing is to get you away from the flour exposure. We'll start you on some inhalers to control your symptoms. I'm also referring you to occupational medicine to document this for workers' compensation - you shouldn't have to bear the cost of a work-related illness.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: If you stay in this job with continued exposure, your asthma will become permanent and won't get better even if you leave later. Early removal from exposure gives the best chance for your airways to recover.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is your livelihood. There may be positions in the bakery with less dust exposure, or you may need to consider a career change. The occupational medicine team can help advocate for workplace modifications or retraining assistance.

guidelineReferences.name: ACCP/ACOEM Occupational Asthma Guidelines
guidelineReferences.citation: Tarlo SM et al. Chest. 2008;134(3 Suppl):1S-41S
keyPoints: Serial peak flow monitoring is gold standard for diagnosis
Early removal from exposure improves prognosis
Continued exposure leads to permanent asthma
Workers should be referred for compensation evaluation
Respiratory protection may reduce but not eliminate risk

guidelineReferences.name: OSHA Respiratory Hazards in Bakeries
guidelineReferences.citation: Occupational Safety and Health Administration
keyPoints: Flour dust is a recognized respiratory sensitizer
10-20% of bakers develop occupational asthma
Engineering controls should minimize dust exposure
Medical surveillance recommended for exposed workers
Baker's asthma is a leading cause of occupational asthma
