male-38-silicosis.name: James Martinez
male-38-silicosis.chiefComplaint: Getting more and more short of breath over the past year, and I can't shake this cough
male-38-silicosis.variantDescription: Young worker with accelerated silicosis from engineered stone countertop fabrication

missedConsequences.Silicosis: Progressive fibrosis leads to respiratory failure. Continued exposure accelerates disease. Patient may qualify for workers' comp.
missedConsequences.Tuberculosis: Silicosis dramatically increases TB risk. Missing TB superinfection leads to treatment delay and spread.

commonPitfalls: Not recognizing engineered stone (quartz countertops) as extremely high silica exposure
Assuming silicosis only affects older workers or miners
Missing that young workers can develop accelerated/acute silicosis with high exposure
Not testing for concurrent TB (silico-tuberculosis)
Failing to remove patient from exposure immediately

allDiagnoses.name: Silicosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Countertop fabrication + progressive dyspnea + restrictive pattern + upper lobe nodules = silicosis.

allDiagnoses.name: Tuberculosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Silicosis massively increases TB risk. Must test - can have silico-TB.

allDiagnoses.name: Sarcoidosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can have similar imaging but occupational history strongly points to silicosis.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Different exposure pattern, but resin fumes could theoretically contribute.

allDiagnoses.name: Lung Cancer
allDiagnoses.rationale: Silicosis is a risk factor for lung cancer - should be considered.

male-38-silicosis.oldcartsNarrative: "I've been cutting and polishing stone countertops for 8 years - mostly quartz these days. About a year ago I started getting short of breath, especially climbing stairs. Now I get winded just walking across the shop floor. I have a cough too - dry, annoying, won't go away. Some of my coworkers have had similar problems. We have dust at work but I thought the masks we use were enough."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your work with countertops
historyQuestions.answer: I cut, grind, and polish quartz countertops. It's dusty work. The quartz is really hard - makes a lot of fine dust when you cut it. I do this 50-60 hours a week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ENGINEERED STONE (quartz countertops) contain 90%+ crystalline silica - EXTREMELY high risk

historyQuestions.text: What respiratory protection do you use?
historyQuestions.answer: We have paper masks but they get wet and uncomfortable. Honestly, most of us don't wear them consistently. There's supposed to be water spray for wet cutting but it's not always working.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Paper masks are inadequate. Need N95 minimum, ideally PAPR. Wet cutting is essential.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned coworkers with problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, two guys I work with are also having breathing problems. One had to quit. He's only 32.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CLUSTER of lung disease in young coworkers = major occupational health concern

historyQuestions.text: How has your breathing changed over time?
historyQuestions.answer: A year ago I noticed it with heavy lifting. Six months ago, stairs got hard. Now I get winded just walking. It's getting worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progressive dyspnea over months = accelerated silicosis (not the slow chronic form)

historyQuestions.text: Any history of TB or exposure to TB?
historyQuestions.answer: Not that I know of. I've never been tested.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Silicosis increases TB risk 30-fold. Must screen.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupational_job_duties.answer: I cut and polish engineered stone (quartz) countertops. Lots of dry cutting, grinding, edge work. Very dusty.
occupational_job_duties.relevance: ENGINEERED STONE is 90%+ crystalline silica - highest silica content of any construction material. This is a PUBLIC HEALTH CRISIS.

occupational_duration.answer: 8 years in this industry. Full time, often 50-60 hours per week.
occupational_duration.relevance: Cumulative dose matters. Long hours without protection = accelerated disease.

occupational_ppe.answer: Paper masks sometimes. No respirator fit testing. Wet cutting system often broken.
occupational_ppe.relevance: INADEQUATE protection. This level of silica exposure requires PAPR or supplied air, mandatory wet cutting.

occupational_coworkers.answer: Two coworkers also have breathing problems. One quit at age 32.
occupational_coworkers.relevance: CLUSTER indicates serious workplace hazard. OSHA/public health should be notified.

physicalExam.finding: BP 124/78, HR 88, RR 22, Temp 98.6°F, SpO2 92% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Hypoxemia at rest indicates significant lung disease

physicalExam.finding: Dry crackles at upper lung zones bilaterally. No wheezes. Decreased breath sounds at apices.
physicalExam.reasoning: Upper zone predominance is characteristic of silicosis

physicalExam.finding: Callused hands. No clubbing currently.
physicalExam.reasoning: Clubbing may develop in advanced disease

physicalExam.finding: Thin male, appears fatigued. Slightly tachypneic at rest.
physicalExam.reasoning: Weight loss and tachypnea indicate disease severity

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Baseline, rule out anemia contributing to dyspnea.

labs.name: TB testing (IGRA preferred)
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL: Silicosis increases TB risk 30-fold! All silicosis patients need TB screening. IGRA preferred over PPD (less false positives).

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Baseline assessment. Renal function important before some medications.

labs.name: ANA, RF (if autoimmune concern)
labs.reasoning: Silicosis can be associated with autoimmune diseases (scleroderma, rheumatoid nodules). Check if clinical suspicion.

labs.name: BNP (if right heart failure suspected)
labs.reasoning: Can develop cor pulmonale from advanced silicosis. Check if signs of right heart failure.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Multiple small nodules in upper and middle lung zones. Hilar lymphadenopathy with eggshell calcifications. Early upper lobe fibrosis with volume loss.
imaging.reasoning: Classic silicosis: upper lobe nodules + eggshell calcification of hilar nodes (nearly pathognomonic). Use ILO classification for reading.

imaging.name: High-resolution CT chest
imaging.result: Profuse upper lobe nodules (2-5mm), some conglomerating. Ground glass changes. Calcified hilar and mediastinal nodes. Progressive massive fibrosis (PMF) beginning to form - conglomerate masses >1cm.
imaging.reasoning: HRCT is more sensitive than CXR. Shows PMF formation - indicates poor prognosis and rapid progression. Essential for staging.

procedures.name: Pulmonary function tests (full PFTs with DLCO)
procedures.result: FVC 62% predicted, FEV1 68%, FEV1/FVC 78% (normal ratio), TLC 68% predicted, DLCO 55% predicted. Restrictive pattern with impaired diffusion.
procedures.reasoning: Documents severity of impairment. Restrictive pattern predominates in silicosis. DLCO reduction indicates parenchymal involvement. Essential for disability evaluation.

procedures.name: Bronchoscopy with BAL (if TB suspected)
procedures.result: BAL with milky appearance. Polarized light shows birefringent silica particles. AFB smear negative x3. AFB cultures pending.
procedures.reasoning: Can directly visualize silica particles under polarized light. Also rules out active pulmonary TB when sputum is negative.

procedures.name: Arterial blood gas
procedures.result: pH 7.42, pCO2 38, pO2 65 on RA (hypoxemia). A-a gradient widened.
procedures.reasoning: Documents hypoxemia. May need supplemental oxygen. Important for disability.

procedures.name: 6-minute walk test
procedures.result: 312 meters (reduced). Desaturation to 85% with exertion.
procedures.reasoning: Objective functional assessment. Desaturation on exertion indicates need for supplemental oxygen with activity.

treatments.name: IMMEDIATE REMOVAL FROM EXPOSURE (CRITICAL)
treatments.result: Patient counseled to stop work immediately. Cannot return to any silica-exposed job. This is the ONLY intervention that slows progression.
treatments.reasoning: MOST IMPORTANT INTERVENTION. Continued exposure accelerates disease - can progress to fatal PMF. No safe exposure level once disease is present.

treatments.name: Latent TB treatment
treatments.result: Started isoniazid 300mg daily + pyridoxine 50mg x 9 months for LTBI. Alternative: rifampin x 4 months.
treatments.reasoning: TREAT LATENT TB in all silicosis patients - 30x increased risk of active TB. Rifampin regimen is shorter but more drug interactions.

treatments.name: Supplemental oxygen
treatments.result: Supplemental oxygen prescribed for exertion - use 2L NC during activity. Ambulatory oxygen testing shows improved walk distance.
treatments.reasoning: Oxygen for exertional desaturation improves function and quality of life. Qualifies for long-term oxygen if PaO2 <55 or <60 with cor pulmonale.

treatments.name: Pneumococcal and influenza vaccination
treatments.result: PPSV23 and PCV20 given. Annual influenza vaccine recommended.
treatments.reasoning: Pneumococcal vaccination essential - silicosis patients have increased risk of pneumococcal disease. Annual flu vaccine also recommended.

treatments.name: Inhaled bronchodilators (if obstructive component)
treatments.result: Tiotropium 18mcg inhaled daily started for mixed restrictive-obstructive pattern.
treatments.reasoning: Some silicosis patients develop airflow obstruction. Trial of bronchodilators may improve symptoms and FEV1.

treatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
treatments.result: Referred to pulmonary rehabilitation program.
treatments.reasoning: Pulmonary rehab improves exercise tolerance, dyspnea, and quality of life in ILD patients. 6-12 week program.

treatments.name: Lung transplant evaluation (if advanced)
treatments.result: This patient's disease is advancing - refer to transplant center for evaluation.
treatments.reasoning: Lung transplant is only cure for advanced silicosis. Early referral important as wait times are long.

consults.name: Pulmonology consult
consults.result: Confirms accelerated silicosis with early PMF. Recommends immediate work removal, LTBI treatment, supplemental oxygen, and pulmonary rehab.
consults.reasoning: Pulmonology for disease management, PFT interpretation, and long-term monitoring.

consults.name: Occupational Medicine consult
consults.result: Documents occupational causation. Initiates workers' compensation claim. Coordinates OSHA notification. Helps patient navigate disability process.
consults.reasoning: ESSENTIAL: Worker needs workers' comp, may qualify for federal Black Lung-type benefits. Workplace needs OSHA investigation.

consults.name: Lung Transplant Center
consults.result: Early referral for transplant evaluation given young age and progressive disease.
consults.reasoning: Only curative option. Young patient with progressive disease should be evaluated early.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with close follow-up
disposition.result: Outpatient management. Pulmonology follow-up in 4-6 weeks. Serial PFTs every 6-12 months to track progression.
disposition.reasoning: Most silicosis managed outpatient. Serial PFTs to monitor for progression.

disposition.name: Immediate and permanent work removal
disposition.result: Cannot return to any job with silica exposure. Ever. Written work restrictions provided.
disposition.reasoning: CRITICAL. Cannot overemphasize - continued exposure will accelerate disease to fatal PMF.

disposition.name: Workers' compensation claim
disposition.result: Occupational medicine assisting with workers' comp claim. Patient may qualify for permanent disability.
disposition.reasoning: Occupational disease - patient entitled to workers' compensation. May qualify for Social Security disability.

disposition.name: OSHA notification
disposition.result: Reportable occupational disease. OSHA notified. Workplace inspection will be scheduled.
disposition.reasoning: Cluster of cases in coworkers indicates serious workplace hazard. OSHA investigation mandatory.

disposition.name: Coworker screening
disposition.result: All workers at this facility should be offered screening (CXR + PFTs). Public health notified.
disposition.reasoning: Coworkers with similar exposure are at high risk. Workplace screening programs can identify early disease.

disposition.name: Long-term monitoring plan
disposition.result: Annual: PFTs, CXR (or CT every 2-3 years), TB screening, oxygen assessment. More frequent if progression.
disposition.reasoning: Disease can progress even after exposure stops. Annual monitoring for progression, TB, and cor pulmonale.

disposition.name: Prognosis counseling
disposition.result: Counseled that silicosis is irreversible and may progress. PMF formation indicates higher risk of rapid decline. Lung transplant may eventually be needed.
disposition.reasoning: Honest discussion of prognosis. Young patient with accelerated silicosis has poor prognosis - median survival with PMF is 5-10 years.

actualDiagnosis.name: Accelerated Silicosis with Latent TB
keyFindings: 8 years of engineered stone (quartz countertop) fabrication
High silica exposure with inadequate protection
Upper lobe nodules and early progressive massive fibrosis on CT
teachingPoints: ENGINEERED STONE (quartz countertops) is 90%+ silica - higher than granite or sandstone
Accelerated silicosis develops in 5-10 years with heavy exposure (vs. chronic: 15-20 years)
This is a PUBLIC HEALTH CRISIS - thousands of countertop workers affected worldwide
Australia has BANNED engineered stone due to silicosis epidemic
Paper masks are USELESS - need N95 minimum, preferably PAPR, with wet cutting
Silicosis increases TB risk 30-fold - MUST screen all silicosis patients
No treatment reverses silicosis - prevention and exposure removal are paramount
Young workers dying of 'old man's disease' - recognize this new occupational epidemic

closureScript.diagnosis: You have silicosis - lung disease caused by breathing in silica dust from cutting those quartz countertops. The quartz you work with is over 90% silica, and over the years that dust has caused scarring in your lungs. You also have latent TB infection, which we need to treat because silicosis makes you very susceptible to tuberculosis.
closureScript.plan: First and most important - you cannot go back to that job. Ever. Continued exposure will make this worse. We're going to start you on treatment for the latent TB to prevent it from becoming active. You'll need regular lung function tests to monitor progression.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: I have to be honest - this disease doesn't go away. We can prevent it from getting worse by stopping exposure, but the damage that's done is permanent. If you develop a cough with blood, fever, or rapidly worsening shortness of breath, come in immediately.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is devastating news about your career. You should qualify for workers' compensation. I'm also obligated to report this to occupational health authorities - your coworkers are at risk and that workplace needs inspection. This isn't your fault - you weren't properly protected.

guidelineReferences.name: OSHA Respirable Crystalline Silica Standard 29 CFR 1926.1153
guidelineReferences.citation: Occupational Safety and Health Administration
keyPoints: PEL: 50 μg/m³ respirable crystalline silica TWA
Engineered stone contains 90%+ silica (highest risk)
Wet cutting methods required to reduce dust
Respiratory protection program mandatory
Medical surveillance every 3 years

guidelineReferences.name: CDC/NIOSH Health Alert: Silicosis in Countertop Workers 2019
guidelineReferences.citation: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
keyPoints: Cluster of accelerated silicosis in young countertop workers
Engineered stone more hazardous than natural stone
Inadequate respiratory protection common
Screening recommended for all exposed workers
Australia banned engineered stone cutting 2024

male-52-berylliosis.name: Daniel Patterson
male-52-berylliosis.chiefComplaint: I've had a dry cough for months and I'm getting more short of breath
male-52-berylliosis.variantDescription: Chronic beryllium disease in aerospace manufacturing worker

missedConsequences.Chronic Beryllium Disease: Progressive fibrosis leading to respiratory failure. Continued exposure worsens disease. Early removal can halt progression.
missedConsequences.Sarcoidosis: Missing sarcoidosis delays treatment with steroids.
missedConsequences.Lung Cancer: Beryllium is a carcinogen - increased lung cancer risk.

commonPitfalls: Diagnosing as sarcoidosis without asking about beryllium exposure
Not recognizing the industries with beryllium exposure (aerospace, defense, electronics, dental)
Missing that CBD is a hypersensitivity reaction, not just toxicity
Not ordering beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test (BeLPT)
Failing to screen coworkers for beryllium sensitization

allDiagnoses.name: Chronic Beryllium Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Aerospace worker + granulomatous lung disease + occupational beryllium exposure = CBD until proven otherwise.

allDiagnoses.name: Sarcoidosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Looks identical to sarcoidosis on imaging and biopsy. Must differentiate by exposure history and BeLPT.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Different mechanism but can have similar presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Tuberculosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Granulomas on imaging require TB rule-out.

male-52-berylliosis.oldcartsNarrative: "I've had this nagging dry cough for about 6 months that won't go away. Over the past couple months I've noticed I get more winded climbing stairs. I work in aerospace manufacturing - have for 25 years. We machine beryllium components for satellites. Recently had a chest X-ray for something else and my doctor said there were some spots that needed follow-up."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me exactly what you do at work
historyQuestions.answer: I machine beryllium alloy components for satellites and aerospace equipment. Been doing it 25 years. Beryllium is really light and stiff - perfect for space applications.
historyQuestions.reasoning: BERYLLIUM EXPOSURE is the key. Machining generates respirable particles.

historyQuestions.text: What kind of dust exposure do you have?
historyQuestions.answer: There's definitely dust when we machine the parts. We have ventilation and I wear a respirator, but older workers tell stories about how dusty it was in the old days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Even with modern controls, some exposure occurs. Historical exposures may be significant.

historyQuestions.text: Any coworkers with similar problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, actually. Two guys I know were diagnosed with something - they said it was related to beryllium. One had to retire.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cluster in workplace confirms occupational exposure concern

historyQuestions.text: How have your symptoms changed over time?
historyQuestions.answer: The cough has been slowly getting worse. At first just occasional, now pretty persistent. The breathing thing is newer - past 2 months.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual progression typical of CBD

historyQuestions.text: Any other symptoms like fevers, weight loss, skin problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Lost maybe 10 pounds over the past year. No fevers. No skin issues.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Systemic symptoms occur in both CBD and sarcoidosis

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupational_industry.answer: Aerospace manufacturing - machining beryllium alloy components for satellites. 25 years in the industry.
occupational_industry.relevance: BERYLLIUM EXPOSURE is the critical factor. CBD is caused by immunologic sensitization to beryllium, not just toxic exposure.

occupational_ppe.answer: We use respirators and have local exhaust ventilation. But older workers had much more exposure before modern controls.
occupational_ppe.relevance: Even low-level exposure can cause sensitization. Historical practices often had minimal protection.

occupational_coworkers.answer: Two coworkers diagnosed with beryllium-related disease. One had to retire early.
occupational_coworkers.relevance: Cluster of cases in workplace confirms significant exposure hazard.

occupational_duration.relevance: Long duration increases cumulative exposure, though even brief intense exposure can sensitize.

physicalExam.finding: BP 132/82, HR 78, RR 18, Temp 98.6°F, SpO2 94% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Mild hypoxemia at rest indicates significant pulmonary involvement

physicalExam.finding: Fine inspiratory crackles at lung bases. No wheezes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Basilar crackles consistent with interstitial lung disease

physicalExam.finding: No skin lesions or rashes
physicalExam.reasoning: Skin granulomas can occur in both CBD and sarcoidosis

physicalExam.finding: No uveitis
physicalExam.reasoning: Uveitis more common in sarcoidosis

physicalExam.finding: No peripheral lymphadenopathy
physicalExam.reasoning: Peripheral nodes less common in CBD than sarcoidosis

labs.name: Beryllium Lymphocyte Proliferation Test (BeLPT)
labs.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC for beryllium sensitization. Differentiates CBD from sarcoidosis.

labs.name: CBC and CMP
labs.reasoning: Baseline studies

labs.name: ACE level
labs.reasoning: Elevated in both CBD and sarcoidosis - doesn't differentiate

labs.name: Calcium level
labs.reasoning: Hypercalcemia can occur in granulomatous diseases

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Reticulonodular infiltrates in mid and lower lung zones.
imaging.reasoning: Classic pattern - identical to sarcoidosis

imaging.name: High-resolution CT chest
imaging.result: Hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Scattered small nodules along bronchovascular bundles. Ground glass opacities.
imaging.reasoning: Better characterization - perilymphatic nodules suggest granulomatous disease

procedures.name: Pulmonary function tests
procedures.result: Restrictive pattern: FVC 72%, DLCO 65%
procedures.reasoning: Documents severity and helps monitor progression

procedures.name: Bronchoscopy with BAL
procedures.result: BAL lymphocytosis (40%). BAL BeLPT positive. Transbronchial biopsy shows non-caseating granulomas.
procedures.reasoning: BAL BeLPT is highly sensitive. Biopsy confirms granulomatous disease.

consults.name: Pulmonology consult
consults.result: Confirms CBD. Recommends prednisone if symptomatic progression.
consults.reasoning: Pulmonology for ongoing management

consults.name: Occupational Medicine consult
consults.result: Documents occupational causation. Coordinates with employer for workplace evaluation.
consults.reasoning: Workplace needs evaluation. Worker may need reassignment.

disposition.name: Removal from beryllium exposure
disposition.result: Patient counseled to discuss job reassignment with employer. Cannot continue beryllium work.
disposition.reasoning: Continued exposure worsens disease in sensitized individuals

actualDiagnosis.name: Chronic Beryllium Disease (CBD)
keyFindings: 25 years of beryllium machining in aerospace
teachingPoints: CBD is clinically and pathologically IDENTICAL to sarcoidosis - only beryllium exposure history and BeLPT differentiate them
CBD is a cell-mediated (Type IV) hypersensitivity, not simple toxicity
Industries with beryllium: aerospace, defense, nuclear, electronics, dental alloys, ceramics
BeLPT (blood and/or BAL) is the diagnostic test - measures lymphocyte sensitization
Even minimal exposure can sensitize genetically susceptible individuals (HLA-DPB1 association)
Treatment: remove from exposure, steroids for progressive disease
About 2-5% of exposed workers become sensitized, and about half of those develop CBD

closureScript.diagnosis: You have chronic beryllium disease - CBD. It's caused by your immune system reacting to beryllium dust you've breathed in at work over the years. It causes the same kind of inflammation in your lungs as a disease called sarcoidosis, but the cause is different.
closureScript.plan: The most important thing is that you can't continue to work with beryllium. Your employer should be able to reassign you to a position without exposure. We'll monitor your lung function, and if things progress, we can treat with steroids. I'm also referring you to occupational medicine for documentation.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: If you continue to work around beryllium, your disease will get worse. Even with job change, the disease can still progress slowly, so we'll need regular follow-up. If your breathing gets worse or you develop new symptoms, let us know.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: This isn't your fault - many workplaces have beryllium and not everyone knows about the risk. You should qualify for workers' compensation. Your coworkers should also be screened, because people can be sensitized before they have any symptoms.

guidelineReferences.name: ATS Statement: Diagnosis and Management of Beryllium Sensitivity and Chronic Beryllium Disease 2014
guidelineReferences.citation: Balmes JR et al. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2014;190(10):e34-e59
keyPoints: BeLPT is the primary diagnostic test for beryllium sensitization
CBD diagnosis requires sensitization + granulomatous lung disease
Remove from exposure immediately upon diagnosis of sensitization
Corticosteroids for progressive pulmonary disease
Medical surveillance every 1-2 years for exposed workers

guidelineReferences.name: OSHA Beryllium Standard 29 CFR 1910.1024
guidelineReferences.citation: Occupational Safety and Health Administration
keyPoints: Permissible exposure limit (PEL): 0.2 μg/m³ TWA
Action level: 0.1 μg/m³ TWA triggers medical surveillance
Respiratory protection required above PEL
Medical surveillance includes BeLPT testing
Applies to general industry, shipyards, and construction

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination

Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.

male-64-cwp.name: Eric Taylor
male-64-cwp.chiefComplaint: My breathing's been getting worse for years. Now I can barely walk to the mailbox
male-64-cwp.variantDescription: Progressive massive fibrosis from coal mining

missedConsequences.Progressive Massive Fibrosis: End-stage lung disease requiring oxygen, with early mortality. May qualify for Black Lung benefits.
missedConsequences.Tuberculosis: Coal miners have increased TB risk. Missing TB delays treatment.
missedConsequences.Lung Cancer: Coal dust is associated with lung cancer risk.

commonPitfalls: Assuming COPD in a miner without considering pneumoconiosis
Not recognizing that simple CWP can progress to PMF even after exposure ends
Missing the connection between Appalachian origin and coal mining
Not applying for Black Lung benefits for the patient
Not screening for TB (increased risk with silica co-exposure)

allDiagnoses.name: Progressive Massive Fibrosis (Coal Worker's Pneumoconiosis)

allDiagnoses.name: Tuberculosis

male-64-cwp.oldcartsNarrative: "I worked in the coal mines in West Virginia for 35 years. Started when I was 18. Been retired 10 years now but my breathing just keeps getting worse. I'm on oxygen at home. A lot of the guys I worked with have the same thing - some of them are already gone. I get these coughing fits and sometimes bring up black stuff."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your work in the mines
historyQuestions.answer: 35 years underground coal mining. Started in 1979, retired in 2014. Did longwall mining, roof bolting, ran the continuous miner for years. Lots of dust down there.
historyQuestions.reasoning: DECADES of underground coal mining = high coal and silica dust exposure

historyQuestions.text: What was the dust like?
historyQuestions.answer: Thick. Some days you couldn't see 10 feet in front of you. We had masks but half the time they didn't fit right. The older guys never wore them.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High dust exposure with inadequate protection

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned your coworkers?
historyQuestions.answer: A lot of us got sick. Three guys from my crew have already died from black lung. My brother too - he worked the same mine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cluster of severe lung disease in coal mining community

historyQuestions.text: How has your breathing changed over time?
historyQuestions.answer: Started noticing shortness of breath maybe 15 years ago while still working. Got worse over the years. Put me on oxygen 3 years ago. Now I can barely do anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progressive disease even after leaving exposure

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever been tested for black lung?
historyQuestions.answer: Had x-rays through the mine. They always said I had some spots but nothing to worry about. Never got any benefits.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Many miners have abnormal screening but don't receive benefits - should apply

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

occupational_industry.answer: Underground coal mining in West Virginia for 35 years (1979-2014). Longwall mining, continuous miner operation, roof bolting.
occupational_industry.relevance: COAL MINING is the exposure. West Virginia is in the high-risk Central Appalachian region seeing resurgence of severe black lung.

occupational_duration.relevance: Long tenure increases cumulative dose. Disease can progress after exposure stops.

occupational_ppe.answer: Masks provided but often ill-fitting. Older workers didn't use protection. Dust sometimes so thick couldn't see.
occupational_ppe.relevance: Inadequate respiratory protection was common in older mining operations.

geographic_home_location.answer: Central West Virginia - coal mining region.
geographic_home_location.relevance: Central Appalachia is seeing an EPIDEMIC of severe CWP/PMF in miners who worked since 1970s.

physicalExam.finding: BP 138/88, HR 92, RR 24, Temp 98.2°F, SpO2 88% on 2L O2
physicalExam.reasoning: Severe hypoxemia requiring supplemental oxygen

physicalExam.finding: Decreased breath sounds throughout. Coarse crackles at both apices. Poor air movement.
physicalExam.reasoning: Upper zone findings consistent with PMF

physicalExam.finding: Loud P2. JVD present. Trace peripheral edema.
physicalExam.reasoning: Signs of cor pulmonale from chronic hypoxemia

physicalExam.reasoning: Cachexia and respiratory distress from end-stage lung disease

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Polycythemia common in chronic hypoxemia

labs.result: pH 7.36, pCO2 52, pO2 58 on 2L NC. Chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation.
labs.reasoning: Documents severity of respiratory failure

labs.name: TB testing
labs.reasoning: Must rule out TB given upper lobe lesions and silica exposure

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Large bilateral upper lobe masses (>1cm = PMF). Diffuse small nodules. Eggshell calcification of hilar nodes.
imaging.reasoning: Classic PMF appearance. Eggshell calcification suggests silica co-exposure.

imaging.name: CT chest
imaging.result: Bilateral upper lobe conglomerate masses consistent with PMF. Background simple CWP nodules. Emphysema.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms PMF, rules out necrosis/cavitation suggesting TB or cancer

procedures.name: Pulmonary function tests
procedures.result: Severe mixed obstructive-restrictive: FEV1 32%, FVC 45%, DLCO 28%
procedures.reasoning: Documents severity of impairment - severe disease

procedures.name: Echocardiogram
procedures.result: Moderate pulmonary hypertension. RVSP 55. Mild RV dilation.
procedures.reasoning: Cor pulmonale from chronic hypoxemia

consults.name: Pulmonology consult
consults.result: Confirms PMF. Optimizes oxygen therapy. Discusses palliative options.
consults.reasoning: Pulmonology for end-stage lung disease management

consults.name: Black Lung benefits application
consults.result: Application for Federal Black Lung benefits initiated. Patient entitled to medical care and compensation.
consults.reasoning: CRITICAL - many miners don't know they qualify for benefits

disposition.name: Home with increased oxygen
disposition.result: Oxygen increased to 3L continuous. Home health arranged.
disposition.reasoning: Supportive care; no treatment reverses PMF

actualDiagnosis.name: Progressive Massive Fibrosis (Coal Worker's Pneumoconiosis)
keyFindings: Large bilateral upper lobe masses on imaging (PMF)
teachingPoints: Simple CWP = small nodules. PMF = masses >1cm (conglomeration of nodules)
PMF can progress even after exposure stops
Central Appalachia is seeing a RESURGENCE of severe black lung - rates not seen since 1970s
Modern 'super-fine' coal dust from thin seam mining may be more fibrogenic
No treatment reverses CWP/PMF - prevention and exposure reduction are key
Silica is often present in coal mines (rock dusting, cutting through rock) - increases severity
Black Lung Benefits: Federal program provides medical care and compensation to disabled miners
Many miners are denied benefits by company doctors - independent evaluation important

closureScript.diagnosis: You have black lung disease - specifically the advanced form called progressive massive fibrosis. Those 35 years of breathing coal dust caused this. The dust particles built up in your lungs and over time formed scar tissue and those large masses we see on your X-ray.
closureScript.plan: I wish I had better treatment options, but there's no medicine that reverses this damage. We're going to optimize your oxygen to help you feel better and function better. What's really important is that you apply for Black Lung benefits - you've clearly earned them after 35 years in the mines.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Your lung disease is advanced, and we need to be honest that it's going to continue to limit what you can do. If your breathing suddenly gets worse, or you develop fever, come in right away because you'd be at higher risk for infections.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this diagnosis is hard to hear, but you're not alone - this is happening to a lot of miners, especially from your generation. The Black Lung benefits can help cover your medical care and provide compensation. I'll connect you with someone who can help with the application.

guidelineReferences.name: NIOSH Criteria for Coal Mine Dust 2011
guidelineReferences.citation: National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health
keyPoints: Recommended exposure limit: 1 mg/m³ respirable coal mine dust
Progressive massive fibrosis occurs with cumulative exposure
ILO classification system for pneumoconiosis severity
Medical surveillance required for coal miners
No effective treatment - prevention is key

guidelineReferences.name: Federal Black Lung Program - DOL
guidelineReferences.citation: U.S. Department of Labor, Office of Workers' Compensation
keyPoints: Benefits available for miners with CWP/PMF
15+ years mining creates presumption of causation
Covers medical treatment and disability compensation
Survivor benefits available for dependents
Free claims assistance available

male-62-legionella.name: Helen Brooks
male-62-legionella.chiefComplaint: High fever, terrible cough, and I feel confused. Got sick after staying at a hotel for a conference
male-62-legionella.variantDescription: Legionnaires' disease from hotel water system exposure

missedConsequences.Legionnaires' Disease: High mortality (10-25%) if not treated with appropriate antibiotics. Beta-lactams alone are ineffective.
missedConsequences.Community-Acquired Pneumonia: Missing atypical pathogens leads to treatment failure.

commonPitfalls: Not asking about water exposures (hotels, hospitals, hot tubs, cruise ships)
Treating with beta-lactam alone (doesn't cover intracellular organisms)
Missing the GI and neurological symptoms that suggest Legionella
Not ordering Legionella urine antigen test
Failing to report to public health (outbreak potential)

allDiagnoses.name: Legionnaires' Disease

male-62-legionella.oldcartsNarrative: "I was at a conference at a hotel downtown last week. Two days after I got home I started feeling terrible - high fevers, chills, cough. Also had bad diarrhea and stomach cramps. My wife says I've been confused and not making sense. The cough is getting worse and I can barely breathe. I'm a smoker and have diabetes."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the hotel stay
historyQuestions.answer: I was there for 4 days for a business conference. Nice hotel, had a spa and pool. I used the shower in my room daily. Didn't use the pool or spa.
historyQuestions.reasoning: HOTEL WATER SYSTEM exposure - showers generate aerosols that can transmit Legionella

historyQuestions.text: When did symptoms start?
historyQuestions.answer: Checked out of the hotel on Saturday. Started feeling sick Monday - so 2 days later. High fever right away, then the other stuff.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 2-10 day incubation is typical for Legionella

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the diarrhea
historyQuestions.answer: Started same day as the fever. Watery, multiple times a day. Also had crampy stomach pain and nausea.
historyQuestions.reasoning: GI symptoms (diarrhea, abdominal pain) are classic Legionella - distinguishes from typical pneumonia

historyQuestions.text: Has your thinking been affected?
historyQuestions.answer: My wife says I've been saying things that don't make sense. I feel foggy. Can't remember what day it is.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Neurological symptoms (confusion, encephalopathy) are prominent in Legionella

historyQuestions.text: What's your medical history?
historyQuestions.answer: I have diabetes - type 2. I smoke about a pack a day for 40 years. Take metformin and a blood pressure pill.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking and diabetes are major risk factors for Legionella

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

water_exposure_hotel.answer: Stayed 4 days at hotel for conference. Used hotel shower daily. Hotel has spa/pool but didn't use them.
water_exposure_hotel.relevance: HOTEL WATER SYSTEMS are a major source of Legionella outbreaks. Showerheads generate aerosols that can be inhaled.

geographic_recent_travel.answer: Traveled to downtown conference hotel, different city from home.
geographic_recent_travel.relevance: Travel-associated Legionella is common - large buildings with complex water systems are high risk.

water_exposure_other.answer: No hot tub use at home. No other unusual water exposures.
water_exposure_other.relevance: Helps narrow exposure source to hotel.

physicalExam.finding: BP 95/60, HR 118, RR 28, Temp 104.2°F, SpO2 88% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Hypotensive, tachycardic, hypoxic - severe sepsis presentation

physicalExam.finding: Confused, oriented only to person. Difficulty following commands. No focal deficits.
physicalExam.reasoning: Encephalopathy is prominent in Legionnaires' - not just from hypoxia

physicalExam.finding: Decreased breath sounds right lower lobe. Crackles bilateral bases, worse on right. Dullness to percussion RLL.
physicalExam.reasoning: Consolidation pattern - Legionella often causes lobar pneumonia

physicalExam.finding: Mild diffuse tenderness. Hyperactive bowel sounds. No peritoneal signs.
physicalExam.reasoning: GI involvement common in Legionella

physicalExam.finding: HR 118 with temp 104.2°F - relative bradycardia (would expect higher HR for this fever)
physicalExam.reasoning: RELATIVE BRADYCARDIA (pulse-temperature dissociation) is classic Legionella finding

labs.name: Legionella urine antigen
labs.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC - quick turnaround, highly specific. Only detects serogroup 1 (most common).

labs.name: Comprehensive metabolic panel
labs.result: Na 128 (hyponatremia), Cr 1.9 (AKI), AST/ALT mildly elevated, glucose 280
labs.reasoning: HYPONATREMIA is classic for Legionella. Also causes hepatitis and AKI.

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.result: WBC 16.5 with left shift. Normal platelets.
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis common

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.reasoning: Supports bacterial pneumonia

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Elevated lactate indicates sepsis severity

labs.name: COVID-19 PCR
labs.reasoning: Rule out concurrent COVID

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Right lower lobe consolidation with small effusion. Patchy infiltrate left base.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms pneumonia. Legionella often causes rapidly progressive infiltrates.

procedures.name: Start fluoroquinolone or azithromycin IMMEDIATELY
procedures.result: Levofloxacin 750mg IV started
procedures.reasoning: Legionella is INTRACELLULAR - need macrolide or fluoroquinolone. Beta-lactams alone are ineffective.

procedures.name: IV fluid resuscitation
procedures.result: NS bolus for hypotension
procedures.reasoning: Sepsis resuscitation

procedures.name: Supplemental oxygen
procedures.result: 4L NC, improving SpO2
procedures.reasoning: Treat hypoxemia

consults.reasoning: ID for treatment duration and monitoring

consults.name: Public Health notification
consults.result: Reported to health department for outbreak investigation at hotel.
consults.reasoning: MANDATORY - hotel water system needs testing, other cases may exist

disposition.name: ICU admission
disposition.result: Admitted to ICU for severe sepsis monitoring
disposition.reasoning: Hypotension, confusion, hypoxia = ICU level care

actualDiagnosis.name: Legionnaires' Disease
keyFindings: Recent hotel stay (4 days prior to symptom onset)
teachingPoints: Legionella is transmitted by inhaling aerosolized water - NOT person-to-person
Sources: cooling towers, hotel water systems, hot tubs, decorative fountains, hospital water
CLINICAL CLUES: pneumonia + diarrhea + confusion + hyponatremia + relative bradycardia
Legionella urinary antigen is rapid and specific but only detects serogroup 1 (80-90% of cases)
MUST treat with macrolide (azithromycin) or fluoroquinolone - beta-lactams DON'T work
Risk factors: smoking, diabetes, immunosuppression, age >50, chronic lung disease
Report ALL cases to public health - outbreaks require investigation
Travel-associated Legionella is COMMON - always ask about hotels, cruises, hospitals

closureScript.diagnosis: You have Legionnaires' disease - a type of pneumonia caused by bacteria called Legionella. You almost certainly got it from the hotel water system - probably from breathing in tiny water droplets while showering. This bacteria lives in water systems, especially in large buildings.
closureScript.plan: We're admitting you to the ICU because this is a serious infection. You're on IV antibiotics that specifically target this bacteria. You'll also need IV fluids and oxygen. We'll monitor you very closely.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Legionnaires' disease can be severe, especially in smokers and diabetics. With the right antibiotics you should start to improve over the next few days, but this will take time. Your confusion should clear as we treat the infection.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: You can't spread this to others - it's not contagious between people. I've reported this to the health department so they can investigate the hotel water system and make sure no one else gets sick. Were there other people from your conference who got sick?

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

guidelineReferences.name: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021

keyPoints: For high risk of fungal infection: empiric antifungal therapy (Weak)
Immunocompromised patients at high risk for invasive fungal disease
Septic shock: aggressive resuscitation and broad coverage within 1 hour
ARDS management: low tidal volume 6 mL/kg, plateau ≤30 cm H2O
Optimize antifungal dosing based on PK/PD principles

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Aspergillosis Guidelines 2016
guidelineReferences.citation: Patterson TF, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2016;63(4):e1-e60
keyPoints: Voriconazole first-line for invasive pulmonary aspergillosis

child-8-bronx.name: Dylan Williams

child-8-bronx.oldcartsNarrative: "His asthma is out of control - this is his 5th ER visit this year. He's on maximum medications but keeps ending up here. We live in the South Bronx - our apartment is right next to the Major Deegan Expressway. Trucks all night. School is near a bus depot. The apartment has cockroaches and mold from water damage. Landlord won't fix it. When we visit my sister in the suburbs, he's fine."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about where you live.
historyQuestions.answer: South Bronx, apartment building 50 feet from Major Deegan Expressway. Diesel trucks 24/7. Can see the exhaust from his bedroom window.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Traffic proximity = diesel exhaust exposure, major asthma trigger.

historyQuestions.text: Conditions in the apartment?
historyQuestions.answer: Cockroaches - landlord won't exterminate. Water damage in ceiling with mold. Old building, no A/C so windows open in summer. Gas stove for heat when landlord doesn't turn on heat.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Multiple indoor triggers compound outdoor pollution.

historyQuestions.text: Is he better anywhere else?
historyQuestions.answer: When he stays with my sister in Westchester for a week, he's like a different kid. No symptoms, no rescue inhaler.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Environmental control 'trial' shows it's the environment, not the child.

historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.

historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.

historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.

historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

housing_pollution_proximity.answer: 50 feet from Major Deegan Expressway. Constant diesel exhaust exposure.
housing_pollution_proximity.relevance: Traffic pollution is strongest environmental asthma trigger. Living within 150m of highway dramatically increases asthma severity.

housing_indoor_triggers.answer: Cockroaches, mold from water damage, gas stove for supplemental heat
housing_indoor_triggers.relevance: Cockroach allergen is major asthma trigger, especially in urban housing. Mold and gas stoves add to burden.

geographic_zip_code.answer: South Bronx - highest asthma hospitalization rate in nation
geographic_zip_code.relevance: ZIP code determines asthma outcomes more than genetics.

physicalExam.reasoning: Active asthma exacerbation.

treatments.reasoning: Reduces cockroach allergen.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.

missedConsequences.SHS Exposure: Continued asthma exacerbations. Family blamed for 'non-compliance' when they're actually compliant.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral-Induced Wheeze

child-5-apartment.oldcartsNarrative: "Her asthma won't get better no matter what we do. We don't smoke - nobody in our house smokes! But our neighbor smokes 2 packs a day and the smoke comes through the walls and vents. You can smell it. Her cotinine level was elevated at last visit even though we don't smoke. Landlord says there's nothing they can do."

historyQuestions.text: Any smoke exposure?
historyQuestions.answer: WE don't smoke, but neighbor smokes constantly. Smoke comes through walls, electrical outlets, vents. Apartment smells like smoke. Multiple complaints to landlord.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Multi-unit housing smoke infiltration is real and measurable.

historyQuestions.text: Prior testing?
historyQuestions.answer: Cotinine level was elevated last visit. Doctor was confused because we said we don't smoke.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cotinine doesn't lie - proves exposure.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.

physicalExam.finding: Scattered wheezes. Prolonged expiration.

copd-exacerbation.name: Harold M.
copd-exacerbation.variantDescription: Severe COPD exacerbation requiring hospitalization with BiPAP and transition to pulmonary rehabilitation

longitudinalDays.title: Day 0: Emergency Department
longitudinalDays.presentation: 68-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 32%) presents with 4 days of worsening dyspnea, increased sputum (now green). Ran out of albuterol. Using accessory muscles, speaks in 2-3 word phrases.

historyQuestions.text: What's your breathing like on a normal day?
historyQuestions.answer: [Speaks in phrases] I use... oxygen at home... 2 liters. Can walk... to mailbox.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Establishes baseline. Home O2 = severe COPD.

historyQuestions.text: When did this get worse?
historyQuestions.answer: Four days ago... got a cold... then couldn't... catch my breath.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Viral URI triggering exacerbation - most common cause.

historyQuestions.text: What does your sputum look like?
historyQuestions.answer: Was white... now green... lots more of it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Increased volume + purulence = bacterial infection likely.

historyQuestions.text: What inhalers do you use?
historyQuestions.answer: Albuterol... ran out... spiriva... advair too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: On triple therapy. Ran out of rescue inhaler - common trigger.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever been on a breathing machine?
historyQuestions.answer: No... never that bad.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No prior intubation - consider goals of care.

historyQuestions.text: Do you still smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: Quit... 5 years ago... 50 pack-years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 50 pack-year history. Quit 5 years ago.

physicalExam.finding: Elderly man in severe distress. Tripoding. Speaking in 2-3 word phrases. Accessory muscle use.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tripoding, accessory muscles, short phrases = severe exacerbation.

physicalExam.finding: Barrel chest. Accessory muscle use. Paradoxical abdominal breathing.
physicalExam.reasoning: Paradoxical breathing = diaphragm fatigue (concerning).

physicalExam.finding: Diffuse expiratory wheezes. Prolonged expiration (I:E 1:4). Decreased breath sounds at bases. Rhonchi.
physicalExam.reasoning: Wheezes = bronchospasm. Rhonchi = secretions.

labs.name: Arterial blood gas
labs.result: pH 7.28, pCO2 62, pO2 58, HCO3 28. Acute on chronic respiratory acidosis.
labs.reasoning: Elevated CO2 + low pH = hypercapnic respiratory failure.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis suggests infection. Polycythemia from chronic hypoxemia.

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.reasoning: Borderline supports bacterial infection.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Hyperinflated lungs. Flattened diaphragms. No consolidation. No pneumothorax.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out pneumonia, PTX. Confirms severe COPD.

treatments.name: Supplemental oxygen
treatments.result: O2 titrated to SpO2 88-92% (started on 4L NC).
treatments.reasoning: TARGET SpO2 88-92% in COPD! Higher O2 worsens hypercapnia.

treatments.name: Bronchodilator nebulizers
treatments.result: Albuterol 2.5mg + ipratropium 0.5mg nebulizer q20min x 3.
treatments.reasoning: Combination SABA + anticholinergic for bronchospasm.

treatments.name: Systemic corticosteroids
treatments.result: Prednisone 40mg PO.
treatments.reasoning: 5-day steroid course is standard.

treatments.name: Antibiotics
treatments.result: Azithromycin 500mg x1, then 250mg daily x 4 days.
treatments.reasoning: Antibiotics for purulent sputum.

treatments.name: Non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP)
treatments.result: BiPAP: IPAP 12, EPAP 5. Patient tolerating well.
treatments.reasoning: BiPAP for hypercapnic failure (pH <7.35). Reduces intubation by 50%.

disposition.name: Admit to IMC/step-down
disposition.result: Admit to IMC for BiPAP monitoring.
disposition.reasoning: BiPAP requires close monitoring. ICU if worsening.

decisionPoint.question: ABG shows pH 7.28, pCO2 62, pO2 58 on 4L NC. What is the next best step?

options.outcome: Correct! BiPAP for COPD exacerbation with hypercapnic respiratory failure.
Too aggressive. Try BiPAP first unless altered mental status or hemodynamic instability.
Dangerous! High O2 worsens hypercapnia. Target 88-92%.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: BiPAP for COPD exacerbation with pH <7.35 and elevated CO2. Target SpO2 88-92%.

longitudinalDays.title: Day 1: Intermediate Care
overnightEvents: Repeat ABG 4 AM: pH 7.33, pCO2 54 - improving
longitudinalDays.presentation: Harold improving on BiPAP. pH normalizing. Less distressed than yesterday.

historyQuestions.text: How are you tolerating the breathing mask?
historyQuestions.answer: It's uncomfortable... but I can breathe better. Hard to sleep.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Tolerating BiPAP is key to avoiding intubation.

physicalExam.finding: Reduced accessory muscle use. Speaking in longer phrases (4-5 words).
physicalExam.reasoning: Improvement in work of breathing.

labs.name: Repeat ABG
labs.reasoning: pH normalizing. Ready to trial off BiPAP.

labs.name: Sputum culture
labs.reasoning: H. flu common in COPD. Azithromycin appropriate.

treatments.name: BiPAP weaning trial
treatments.result: Trial off BiPAP x 2 hours. Tolerated. SpO2 90% on 4L NC.
treatments.reasoning: Wean BiPAP gradually when pH normalizes.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: BiPAP weaning: Trial off when pH normalizes. Keep on for sleep initially.

longitudinalDays.title: Days 2-3: Medical Floor
longitudinalDays.presentation: Harold transferred to floor. Off BiPAP. Ambulatory with walker. At baseline.

historyQuestions.text: How does your breathing feel?
historyQuestions.answer: Much better. Almost back to my normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Returning to baseline = discharge planning.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your home situation.
historyQuestions.answer: I live with my wife. We have 5 stairs to get in.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Need to ensure safe home environment.

physicalExam.finding: Decreased breath sounds at bases (baseline). Minimal wheezes. No rhonchi.
physicalExam.reasoning: Returning to baseline exam.

physicalExam.finding: Walked 150 feet with walker and O2. SpO2 89-91%. Minimal dyspnea.
physicalExam.reasoning: Adequate for discharge.

treatments.name: Transition to MDIs
treatments.result: Switched to MDIs with spacer. Technique observed.
treatments.reasoning: Ensure can use home inhalers.

consults.name: Case management
consults.result: Home O2 confirmed. Pulmonary rehab referral placed.
consults.reasoning: Pulmonary rehab reduces readmissions by 50%.

consults.name: Pharmacist teaching
consults.result: Inhaler technique reviewed. Teach-back confirmed.
consults.reasoning: Inhaler technique is critical.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: Before discharge: At baseline x 24h, on home O2, tolerating oral meds, inhalers confirmed, rehab referral.

longitudinalDays.title: Day 4: Discharge
longitudinalDays.presentation: Harold at baseline. Ready for discharge.

treatments.name: Discharge medications
treatments.result: Prednisone 40mg x 1 more day. Albuterol PRN. Continue tiotropium and Advair.
treatments.reasoning: Complete 5-day steroid course.

treatments.name: COPD Action Plan
treatments.result: Green zone (stable), Yellow zone (increase nebs, start standby prednisone), Red zone (go to ED).
treatments.reasoning: Action plans reduce exacerbation severity.

disposition.name: Follow-up
disposition.result: PCP in 1 week. Pulmonology in 2 weeks. Pulmonary rehab starts in 3 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Close follow-up reduces readmission.

decisionPoint.question: Which intervention most reduces COPD exacerbation readmissions?

options.text: Pulmonary rehab within 4 weeks of discharge
options.outcome: Correct! Pulmonary rehab reduces readmissions by 50%.
Helpful but less impact than pulmonary rehab.
Not recommended due to side effects.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: Pulmonary rehab within 4 weeks of hospitalization reduces readmissions by 50%.

longitudinalDays.title: Week 2: Pulmonology Follow-up
longitudinalDays.presentation: Harold at pulmonology follow-up. Started pulmonary rehab. Feeling better than before hospitalization.

historyQuestions.text: How is your breathing compared to before?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually better. The rehab is helping. I can walk further.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Early benefit from pulmonary rehab.

historyQuestions.text: How are you managing oxygen at home?
historyQuestions.answer: Good. Concentrator at home, portable tanks for going out. Rehab taught me to manage it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Proper O2 management including portable supply.

treatments.name: Optimize maintenance therapy
treatments.result: Adding roflumilast for exacerbation prevention given severe COPD.
treatments.reasoning: Roflumilast reduces exacerbations in severe COPD.

treatments.name: Standby rescue pack
treatments.result: Prednisone 40mg x 5 days + Z-pack for yellow zone symptoms.
treatments.reasoning: Rescue packs allow early treatment at home.

treatments.name: Vaccinations
treatments.result: Flu vaccine given. PCV20 given. COVID up to date.
treatments.reasoning: Vaccination reduces exacerbations.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: Exacerbation prevention: Triple therapy, pulmonary rehab, vaccinations, action plans.

longitudinalDays.title: Week 6: Post-Pulmonary Rehab
longitudinalDays.presentation: Harold completing pulmonary rehabilitation. Significant improvement.
intervalHistory: Completed 6 weeks of pulmonary rehab (12 sessions)
No longer needs to rest halfway to mailbox

historyQuestions.text: How has the rehabilitation helped you?
historyQuestions.answer: Amazing. I can walk to the mailbox without stopping. Even walked around the block.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Significant functional improvement.

historyQuestions.text: How would you rate your quality of life now?
historyQuestions.answer: So much better. I'm not afraid to leave the house anymore. I can play with my grandkids.
historyQuestions.reasoning: QoL improvement is the primary rehab outcome.

physicalExam.finding: 6MWT: 280 meters (up from 180 meters - 55% improvement). SpO2 maintained 89-92%.
physicalExam.reasoning: >30 meters improvement is clinically meaningful.

labs.name: Pulmonary function tests
labs.reasoning: PFTs may not improve (rehab improves function, not structure).

treatments.name: Maintenance exercise program
treatments.result: Continue exercises at home or maintenance pulmonary rehab class.
treatments.reasoning: Ongoing exercise maintains benefits.

consults.name: Consider palliative care
consults.result: Patient interested in palliative care for symptom management and advance care planning.
consults.reasoning: Palliative care improves QoL in severe COPD.

disposition.name: Ongoing management
disposition.result: Follow-up in 3 months. Annual PFTs. Continue vaccinations.
disposition.reasoning: Long-term management plan.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: Pulmonary rehab improves 6MWT, dyspnea, and QoL but NOT FEV1. Benefits decline without ongoing exercise.

complicationBranch.title: Complication: BiPAP Failure
complicationBranch.trigger: Patient unable to tolerate BiPAP, worsening mental status
complicationBranch.presentation: Harold becomes agitated, pulls at mask. pH drops to 7.22, pCO2 rises to 75. Becomes drowsy.
teachingPoints: BiPAP failure: Worsening pH, altered mental status, unable to protect airway
Don't delay intubation if meeting failure criteria
Ventilator strategy: Long expiratory time, watch for auto-PEEP

actualDiagnosis.name: COPD Exacerbation with Hypercapnic Respiratory Failure
teachingPoints: COPD exacerbation triggers: Infection, medication non-adherence, pollution
BiPAP for hypercapnic failure (pH <7.35) - 50% reduction in intubation
Target SpO2 88-92% in COPD
5-day prednisone, antibiotics for purulent sputum
Pulmonary rehab within 4 weeks reduces readmissions by 50%

copd-exacerbation.clinicalPearl: COPD exacerbation management: (1) Bronchodilators, (2) Steroids x 5 days, (3) Antibiotics if purulent sputum, (4) BiPAP for hypercapnia, (5) O2 to 88-92%. Prevention: Pulmonary rehab is the most effective intervention yet underutilized. Every hospitalized COPD patient should be referred.
