female-32-ocp.name: Maria Santos
female-32-ocp.chiefComplaint: I can't catch my breath - it came on suddenly about 2 hours ago
female-32-ocp.patientVoice: anxious but articulate, breathing rapidly between sentences
female-32-ocp.culturalContext: First-generation Dominican-American, close family, some traditional health beliefs
female-32-ocp.variantDescription: Young woman on OCPs with sudden dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain after long car ride

patientProfile.exerciseHabits: gym 2-3x/week normally, hasn't gone in 2 weeks
patientProfile.diet: tries to eat healthy, occasional fast food
patientProfile.medications: birth control pills (Yaz) x 4 years, multivitamin
patientProfile.familyHistoryType: mother has 'thick blood' - takes blood thinners
patientProfile.obgynHistory: regular periods on OCP, LMP 2 weeks ago, sexually active with boyfriend, no pregnancies

oldcartsNarrative.onset: Sudden onset 2 hours ago while sitting at home watching TV
oldcartsNarrative.location: Feels like tightness in right chest, also feels it when taking deep breaths
oldcartsNarrative.duration: Constant for 2 hours, not getting better
oldcartsNarrative.character: Sharp when breathing deeply, pressure-like at rest. 'Like an elephant sitting on my chest'
oldcartsNarrative.aggravating: Deep breathing, lying flat, any movement makes it worse
oldcartsNarrative.relieving: Sitting upright and leaning forward helps slightly

patientBackground.recentEvents: Maria just returned from an 8-hour car trip to visit her parents in another state. She drove most of the way and only stopped once for gas. Started her new birth control pills 3 months ago. Has been sitting at her desk job more than usual due to a big project deadline.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: Completely healthy until this morning. No recent illnesses, surgeries, or injuries. Was at baseline exercising at the gym 3 times per week until this busy work period.
patientBackground.fluidIntake: Drinks coffee in the morning, tries to drink water throughout the day but admits she does not drink enough, especially when busy at work.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Works as a marketing manager at a tech company - high stress, long hours sitting at computer. Recently took on extra responsibilities. Her older sister had a blood clot during pregnancy - Maria is now worried this might run in the family.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Lives with her fiance who is very supportive. Parents live out of state but checking in frequently by phone. Close friends from work. Planning wedding for next year.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Maria is health-conscious and proactive. She researched her symptoms online before coming in and is worried about a blood clot. She wants to understand everything about her condition and treatment options. Prefers to be involved in medical decisions.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Has not been to the ER since she was a child. Generally very healthy. Gets annual checkups and is up to date on preventive care.
patientBackground.occupation: Marketing manager at a tech startup. Good health insurance. Can work remotely if needed.

historyQuestions.text: Can you tell me exactly what happened when this started?
historyQuestions.answer: I was just sitting on the couch watching Netflix, and suddenly I felt like I couldn't get a full breath. At first I thought maybe I was having a panic attack, but this feels different - it actually hurts.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sudden onset at rest is classic for PE. Patient correctly distinguishes this from anxiety.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the chest pain.
historyQuestions.answer: It's sharp, right here on the right side of my chest. It gets worse when I take a deep breath or cough. When I'm just sitting still it's more like a pressure feeling.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pleuritic chest pain (worse with inspiration) is present in ~50% of PE cases

historyQuestions.text: How bad is the shortness of breath?
historyQuestions.answer: It's scary bad. I can't take a full breath without it hurting. I feel like I've been running a marathon but I've just been sitting here. Even talking is making me winded.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dyspnea out of proportion to exam findings is concerning for PE

historyQuestions.text: Any cough?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I've had a dry cough. Earlier when I coughed hard, I noticed a tiny bit of blood in my tissue. That freaked me out.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hemoptysis occurs in 20-30% of PE cases. Even small amount is significant.

historyQuestions.text: Any pain or swelling in your legs?
historyQuestions.answer: Now that you mention it, my left calf has been kind of achy since we got back from the trip. I thought it was just from sitting in the car so long. It's a little swollen compared to the other one.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Concurrent DVT symptoms present in 30-50% of PE patients. This is a critical finding.

historyQuestions.text: Did you pass out or feel like you might?
historyQuestions.answer: I got really lightheaded when I stood up to go to the bathroom. I had to grab the wall. I didn't actually pass out but I felt like I might.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Near-syncope suggests hemodynamic compromise and more severe PE

historyQuestions.text: Any heart racing or palpitations?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, my heart has been pounding since this started. I can feel it beating fast even when I'm sitting still.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Tachycardia is one of the most common signs of PE

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had blood clots before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never. But my mom had a blood clot in her leg a few years ago. She said she has 'thick blood' and has to take blood thinners now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of VTE suggests possible inherited thrombophilia (Factor V Leiden, etc)

historyQuestions.text: What birth control are you using?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been on the pill - Yaz - for about 4 years. I started it for acne and period cramps.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Combined OCPs increase VTE risk 3-4x. Drospirenone (in Yaz) may have even higher risk.

historyQuestions.text: Have you been traveling or sitting for long periods recently?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! We drove to North Carolina last weekend - it's like 8 hours each way. We only stopped once because we were trying to get there fast. And at work I've been chained to my desk for this big project.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prolonged immobility (travel >4 hours) is a major VTE risk factor

historyQuestions.text: Any recent surgeries or hospitalizations?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing recent. Just had my wisdom teeth out years ago. Never been in the hospital otherwise.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent surgery is a major VTE risk factor, but not present here

historyQuestions.text: Any history of cancer?
historyQuestions.answer: No, thank God. My grandmother had breast cancer but she's okay now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Malignancy is a major VTE risk factor. Family history noted.

historyQuestions.text: Any chance you could be pregnant?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't think so - I'm on the pill and we're careful. My last period was about 2 weeks ago and it was normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Must rule out pregnancy before CT with contrast. Pregnancy itself is a VTE risk factor.

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never. My boyfriend vapes but I've never smoked anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking adds to VTE risk, especially with OCPs

historyQuestions.text: Are you taking any other medications?
historyQuestions.answer: Just a multivitamin and sometimes ibuprofen for headaches. Oh, and I've been taking DayQuil for that cold I had.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Complete medication history important for drug interactions with anticoagulation

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: None that I know of.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before administering contrast or anticoagulation

historyQuestions.text: Any headache, vision changes, or weakness?
historyQuestions.answer: No headache or vision problems. I feel weak all over but nothing like one side is different from the other.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out stroke, which can present with dyspnea in some cases

historyQuestions.text: Any nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain?
historyQuestions.answer: I felt a little nauseous when this first started but I haven't thrown up. No belly pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nausea can occur with significant PE

historyQuestions.text: Any fevers or chills?
historyQuestions.answer: I've felt a little warm but no real fever. No chills.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Low-grade fever can occur with PE or suggest pneumonia

historyQuestions.text: What have you been doing the past few days?
historyQuestions.answer: Mostly working - sitting at my desk. We got back from the road trip Monday night. Tuesday and Wednesday I was exhausted and just went to bed early. Today I was finally starting to feel normal and then this happened.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline suggests clot may have formed during travel and embolized days later

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had panic attacks?
historyQuestions.answer: I get anxious sometimes but never like this. I've had moments where I felt stressed and my heart raced, but I could always calm myself down. This is completely different - the pain is real and I can't make it go away.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important to differentiate from anxiety, but patient clearly distinguishes this episode

historyQuestions.text: What are you most worried about?
historyQuestions.answer: Honestly? I'm scared I'm having a heart attack or something is really wrong with my lungs. My cousin had a blood clot and almost died. Is that what this could be?
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient concern about PE is appropriate given family history and symptoms

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about your mother's blood clot.
historyQuestions.answer: She got it in her leg about 5 years ago. They said it was a DVT. She was on blood thinners for a while and they tested her blood - apparently she has some genetic thing that makes her more likely to clot. They tested me too when it happened but I was only 27.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms inherited thrombophilia in family. Patient may have same condition.

historyQuestions.text: Any other family history I should know about?
historyQuestions.answer: My dad has high blood pressure. My grandmother had breast cancer. That's about it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Complete family history for documentation

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your work.
historyQuestions.answer: I'm a marketing coordinator. Lots of computer work, sitting in meetings, that kind of thing. The past two weeks have been insane - I've barely gotten up from my desk except to go to the bathroom.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sedentary occupation contributes to VTE risk

historyQuestions.text: Do you exercise?
historyQuestions.answer: Usually I go to the gym 2-3 times a week, but I haven't been in like 2 weeks because of work. I was planning to get back to it next week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent decrease in activity may contribute to risk

physicalExamFindings.finding: Anxious-appearing young woman in moderate respiratory distress, sitting upright, speaking in short sentences
physicalExamFindings.interpretation: Significant distress, cannot tolerate lying flat

physicalExamFindings.finding: Tachypneic with accessory muscle use. No chest wall abnormalities.
Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Slightly decreased breath sounds at right base.
physicalExamFindings.interpretation: Clear lungs but decreased sounds could indicate effusion or atelectasis from PE

physicalExamFindings.finding: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Normal S1/S2. Loud P2 component. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
physicalExamFindings.interpretation: Loud P2 suggests pulmonary hypertension - concerning for significant PE
Elevated JVP suggests right heart strain from PE

physicalExamFindings.finding: Left calf is 3 cm larger in circumference than right. Mild warmth and tenderness to palpation along the course of the deep veins. Positive Homans sign (calf pain with dorsiflexion). Trace pitting edema. No erythema.
physicalExamFindings.interpretation: Classic DVT findings - swelling, warmth, tenderness, positive Homans

physicalExamFindings.finding: Right leg normal, no swelling or tenderness
Warm, pale. No cyanosis. Capillary refill 2 seconds.
Alert and oriented x3. Anxious but appropriate. No focal deficits.

mcqs.question: This 32-year-old woman on OCPs presents with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, and unilateral leg swelling after a long car trip. Her mother has a history of DVT and a clotting disorder. Using the Wells criteria for PE, what is her risk stratification and next step?

options.text: Low probability - apply PERC rule to determine if workup needed
options.explanation: Incorrect. She has multiple Wells criteria points: clinical signs of DVT (+3), PE is most likely diagnosis (+3), tachycardia (+1.5), immobilization (+1.5) = 9+ points, which is HIGH probability.

options.text: Moderate probability - order D-dimer, proceed to CTPA if positive
options.explanation: Incorrect. With Wells score >6, this is HIGH probability PE. D-dimer is not useful here - it will be positive and doesn't change management. Go directly to imaging.

options.text: High probability - proceed directly to CTPA and consider empiric anticoagulation
options.explanation: Correct! Wells score >6 is HIGH probability. D-dimer should NOT be used to rule out PE in high-probability patients. Proceed directly to CTPA. Given high clinical suspicion, consider starting anticoagulation while awaiting imaging if no contraindications.
Incorrect. Thrombolytics are reserved for massive PE with hemodynamic instability. She is tachycardic but has stable blood pressure. Need imaging confirmation first.

mcqs.question: CTPA confirms bilateral pulmonary emboli with right heart strain (RV/LV ratio 1.2). Troponin is mildly elevated at 0.15 ng/mL. BNP is 450 pg/mL. She remains hemodynamically stable (BP 118/76). How should she be risk-stratified and what is the initial treatment?

options.text: Low-risk PE - outpatient treatment with DOAC is appropriate
options.explanation: Incorrect. RV dysfunction on imaging plus elevated troponin and BNP = intermediate-HIGH risk PE. This requires ICU admission and close monitoring, not outpatient treatment.

options.text: Intermediate-high risk PE - admit to ICU, start anticoagulation, monitor for decompensation
options.explanation: Correct! She has intermediate-high risk PE: hemodynamically stable BUT with RV dysfunction (RV/LV >1.0) AND elevated cardiac biomarkers. Start anticoagulation (DOAC or heparin), ICU admission for monitoring. If she decompensates (hypotension), escalate to thrombolytics or catheter-directed therapy.

options.text: High-risk (massive) PE - immediate systemic thrombolysis indicated
options.explanation: Incorrect. High-risk/massive PE requires hemodynamic instability (sustained hypotension, shock). She is stable despite RV strain. Thrombolytics carry bleeding risk and are not first-line for stable patients.

options.text: Intermediate-low risk PE - admit to floor, anticoagulation alone
options.explanation: Intermediate-LOW would have RV dysfunction OR elevated biomarkers, not both. She has BOTH, making this intermediate-HIGH risk requiring closer monitoring than a regular floor bed.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.reasoning: Classic presentation with multiple risk factors. Wells score >6 (high probability).

allDiagnoses.name: Deep Vein Thrombosis
allDiagnoses.reasoning: Source of pulmonary embolism. Needs ultrasound confirmation.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumothorax
allDiagnoses.reasoning: Can cause sudden dyspnea and pleuritic pain, but leg symptoms and risk factors point to PE

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.reasoning: Recent URI history but sudden onset and leg symptoms make PE more likely

allDiagnoses.name: Panic Attack
allDiagnoses.reasoning: Patient clearly distinguishes this from previous anxiety. Hemoptysis and leg symptoms cannot be explained by panic.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Coronary Syndrome
allDiagnoses.reasoning: 32-year-old woman without cardiac risk factors. Pleuritic nature and leg symptoms point away from ACS.

teachingPoints: Wells criteria for PE: High probability (>6), Moderate (2-6), Low (<2)
Don't use D-dimer to rule out PE in high-probability patients - go directly to CTPA
The triad of dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and hemoptysis is present in <20% of PE cases
Combined OCPs increase VTE risk 3-4 fold; family history of thrombophilia increases this further
Risk stratification determines treatment intensity: low (outpatient), intermediate-low (floor), intermediate-high (ICU), high (thrombolytics)
RV dysfunction + elevated biomarkers = intermediate-HIGH risk even if hemodynamically stable
criticalActions: Recognize high-probability PE and skip D-dimer
Order CTPA (or V/Q if contrast contraindicated)
Check RV function and cardiac biomarkers for risk stratification
Start anticoagulation (consider empiric treatment while awaiting imaging)
Evaluate both legs for DVT source

missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: Untreated PE has 30% mortality. Clot propagation, recurrent embolism, hemodynamic collapse, and death can occur rapidly.
missedConsequences.Deep Vein Thrombosis: Untreated DVT leads to further embolization. Also causes chronic post-thrombotic syndrome with pain, swelling, and ulceration.

commonPitfalls: Attributing symptoms to anxiety in young women - PE can occur at any age with risk factors
Using D-dimer in high-probability patients - wastes time and doesn't change management
Missing leg symptoms - always examine both legs and ask about swelling/pain
Underestimating severity - stable BP doesn't mean low risk if RV strain present
Forgetting to ask about OCP use, travel, immobility
Not considering family history of clotting disorders
Delaying anticoagulation while waiting for imaging in high-probability cases

imagingAvailable.CXR: Low lung volumes. Subtle wedge-shaped opacity at right base (Hampton hump). No pneumothorax. Heart size normal.
imagingAvailable.CTPA: Filling defects in right main pulmonary artery extending into right upper and lower lobar arteries. Smaller filling defect in left lower lobe segmental artery. RV/LV ratio 1.2 indicating right ventricular strain. No pericardial effusion.
imagingAvailable.Lower Extremity Duplex: Acute thrombus in left common femoral vein extending into superficial femoral vein. No thrombus on right.
imagingAvailable.ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 112. S1Q3T3 pattern present. T-wave inversions in V1-V3. No ST elevation.

treatmentsAvailable.Rivaroxaban: 15mg BID with food x21 days, then 20mg daily
treatmentsAvailable.tPA: Reserved for massive PE with hemodynamic instability

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

pleural-effusion-primary.name: Patient
pleural-effusion-primary.chiefComplaint: Patient with progressive dyspnea and dullness to percussion at lung base
pleural-effusion-primary.variantDescription: Light criteria, transudative vs exudative, thoracentesis analysis, common etiologies
keyFindings: Light criteria (EXUDATE if ANY met): Pleural protein/serum protein >0.5; Pleural LDH/serum LDH >0.6; Pleural LDH >2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH
Transudative: CHF (most common overall), cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome — systemic factors
Exudative: infection (parapneumonic/empyema), malignancy, PE, TB, autoimmune — local factors
Thoracentesis: cell count, protein, LDH, glucose, pH, cytology, cultures
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Pleural Effusion
Delayed diagnosis
Inadequate workup

options.text: Light criteria (EXUDATE if ANY met): Pleural protein/serum protein >0.5; Pleural LDH/serum LDH >0.6; Pleural LDH >2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH
options.explanation: This is the key concept.
Further evaluation is warranted.

questions.stem: Pulmonary function tests show: FEV1 55% predicted, FVC 90% predicted, FEV1/FVC ratio 0.61, significant bronchodilator response (+15% FEV1). What is the interpretation?

options.text: Moderate obstructive lung disease with reversibility, consistent with asthma
options.explanation: FEV1/FVC <0.70 confirms obstruction. FEV1 50-79% predicted = moderate severity (GOLD/GINA). Significant bronchodilator response (≥12% AND ≥200mL improvement in FEV1) suggests reversible airway obstruction characteristic of asthma. COPD typically shows less reversibility.

options.text: Restrictive lung disease based on reduced FEV1
options.explanation: Restrictive disease shows reduced FVC with PRESERVED or elevated FEV1/FVC ratio (≥0.70). Here, FVC is 90% (normal) and FEV1/FVC is 0.61 (reduced), indicating obstruction, not restriction.

options.text: Normal spirometry — FVC is within normal limits
options.explanation: Normal FVC does not rule out obstructive disease. The FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.61 (<0.70 threshold) definitively identifies airflow obstruction.

options.text: Fixed airway obstruction consistent with COPD
options.explanation: A 15% bronchodilator response indicates significant REVERSIBILITY, which argues against fixed obstruction. COPD typically shows <12% improvement post-bronchodilator.

respiratory-pft-primary.name: Patient
respiratory-pft-primary.chiefComplaint: Smoker with dyspnea on exertion referred for pulmonary function testing
respiratory-pft-primary.variantDescription: FEV1/FVC ratio, obstructive vs restrictive patterns, DLCO interpretation, flow-volume loops
keyFindings: Obstructive: FEV1/FVC <0.70 (COPD, asthma); air trapping → increased TLC, RV
Restrictive: FEV1/FVC normal or increased, but reduced FVC and TLC (ILD, chest wall disease, neuromuscular)
Reversibility: >12% AND >200mL increase in FEV1 post-bronchodilator = significant (supports asthma)
DLCO decreased: emphysema, ILD, pulmonary HTN, anemia; DLCO increased: polycythemia, pulmonary hemorrhage, asthma
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Respiratory PFTs
Delayed diagnosis
Inadequate workup

mcqs.question: What is the most important consideration in this case?

options.text: Obstructive: FEV1/FVC <0.70 (COPD, asthma); air trapping → increased TLC, RV
options.explanation: This is the key concept.
